reason: Within an already ≥80 cohort, incremental age adds limited additional discrimination, though frailty/renal reserve generally worsen with age. Kept low to preserve sparsity.

Sex differences in muscle mass and baseline creatinine kinetics may modestly affect creatinine ratio, but it is not a strong AKI driver post-cardiac surgery. Likely indirect/confounded by body size and comorbidity.
Race is not a direct physiologic driver of postoperative AKI and often acts as a proxy for social/structural factors. Minimal incremental value for creatinine ratio in this setting.

Black race can correlate with differences in baseline kidney disease burden and creatinine generation, which may modestly influence postoperative creatinine trajectories. Still indirect and not a direct perioperative AKI mechanism.
No strong direct mechanistic link to post-cardiac surgery AKI beyond confounding by comorbidities and body habitus. Kept very low for sparsity.
May proxy higher CKD/diabetes prevalence in some populations, which can increase AKI susceptibility. Indirect and likely underpowered, so low.
Potential indirect association via comorbidity patterns (e.g., diabetes/CKD) rather than direct perioperative renal injury mechanisms. Low importance to avoid overfitting.
Heterogeneous category with no specific physiologic linkage to AKI risk. Minimal expected predictive value.
Ethnicity is not a direct biomarker or hemodynamic driver of AKI and mainly reflects confounding factors. Kept at minimal inclusion probability.

Height has little direct relationship to renal perfusion injury; any effect is mediated through body size and creatinine generation. Minimal incremental value once weight/comorbidities are considered.
Higher weight/obesity can increase AKI risk via hemodynamic stress, inflammation, and dosing/volume management complexity after cardiac surgery. Still an indirect risk factor, so kept low.
Family history is not mechanistically linked to postoperative renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxic exposure. Unlikely to add predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Diabetes is an established risk factor for perioperative AKI due to microvascular disease and reduced renal reserve, especially after cardiopulmonary bypass. It plausibly increases susceptibility to creatinine rise by hour 60.
Glycemic control status may reflect chronic disease severity and microvascular burden, but is an indirect and variably defined predictor. Lower importance than diabetes itself to reduce redundancy.
Dyslipidemia is not a direct driver of acute postoperative renal injury and is often a marker of cardiovascular risk rather than renal perfusion. Minimal expected contribution.

Hypertension is an established AKI susceptibility factor via chronic nephrosclerosis and impaired autoregulation, making kidneys more vulnerable to perioperative hypotension/CPB. Moderate but not definitive predictor.
Prior endocarditis can be associated with chronic inflammation, embolic phenomena, and more complex surgery, indirectly increasing AKI risk. It is not a direct perioperative renal perfusion marker.

Chronic lung disease can predispose to hypoxemia/hypercapnia and higher ventilatory/vasopressor needs, indirectly affecting renal perfusion. Association with AKI exists but is not a primary driver compared with hemodynamics and nephrotoxins.

Home oxygen is a proxy for severe chronic pulmonary disease and limited physiologic reserve, which can correlate with postoperative instability and AKI. It is an indirect risk marker rather than a mechanistic renal biomarker.
Bronchodilator use mainly reflects obstructive lung disease management and has no specific causal link to postoperative AKI. Any association is largely confounded by underlying pulmonary severity.

Sleep apnea is associated with cardiometabolic comorbidity and intermittent hypoxemia, which may modestly increase perioperative risk. It is not a strong or direct predictor of creatinine rise after cardiac surgery.

Perioperative pneumonia can drive sepsis physiology, hypoxemia, and vasopressor exposure, all of which can precipitate AKI. Still, it is an indirect marker compared with direct hemodynamic and renal-function variables.
IV drug use may correlate with infections and social factors but is not a consistent physiologic predictor of postoperative AKI in this setting. Any effect is indirect and heterogeneous.
Depression has no direct physiologic linkage to acute postoperative renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity. It is unlikely to add predictive value beyond clinical severity variables.
Alcohol use can be associated with liver disease, malnutrition, and hemodynamic instability, which may modestly increase AKI risk. On its own it is an indirect and inconsistently measured predictor.

Liver disease is associated with baseline circulatory dysfunction, susceptibility to hypotension, and higher risk of postoperative complications that can reduce renal perfusion. It is an established comorbidity risk factor for AKI after major surgery.

Immunosuppression increases infection risk and may involve nephrotoxic agents (e.g., calcineurin inhibitors), indirectly raising AKI risk. The relationship is variable and not as direct as perioperative hemodynamics.
Prior mediastinal radiation can make cardiac surgery more complex and increase bypass time/bleeding risk, indirectly affecting AKI. It is not a direct renal injury marker.
Cancer can reflect frailty, inflammation, and exposure to nephrotoxic therapies, but effects are heterogeneous. It is generally an indirect predictor compared with immediate postoperative physiology.

Peripheral artery disease is a marker of systemic atherosclerosis and microvascular disease, which is associated with higher AKI risk after cardiac surgery. It is an established comorbidity risk factor but not definitive alone.

Thoracic aortic disease often implies more complex operations (longer cross-clamp/CPB, embolic risk) and greater hemodynamic perturbations, increasing AKI risk. It is an established perioperative risk factor.

History of syncope is nonspecific and does not directly indicate renal vulnerability or postoperative renal hypoperfusion. It is unlikely to contribute meaningfully once surgical and ICU physiology are accounted for.
History of unresponsiveness is not a direct renal perfusion or nephrotoxin exposure marker and is unlikely to add specific AKI signal beyond overall frailty/illness severity.
Cerebrovascular disease is a marker of systemic atherosclerosis and vascular stiffness, which can correlate with perioperative hypoperfusion risk, but it is an indirect predictor of AKI.
Pre-op anemia increases risk of perioperative transfusion and reduced oxygen delivery, both associated with post-cardiac surgery AKI, though it is not a definitive driver alone.
Low albumin reflects frailty/inflammation and lower oncotic pressure (worse effective circulating volume), which can predispose to AKI, but remains an indirect risk marker.
Elevated bilirubin suggests hepatic dysfunction/congestion and higher perioperative risk, but its relationship to AKI is indirect and confounded by overall illness severity.
Higher HbA1c indicates diabetes/chronic glycemic burden and microvascular disease, an established risk factor for postoperative AKI, though not a direct biomarker.
HIT antibodies mainly affect anticoagulation strategy and thrombosis risk; they are not a typical direct predictor of postoperative creatinine rise in this setting.
Higher MELD (including creatinine component) reflects hepatorenal vulnerability and worse baseline organ reserve, which is associated with higher AKI risk after cardiac surgery.
Elevated BNP indicates heart failure and venous congestion, which can reduce renal perfusion gradient and is a recognized risk factor for cardiorenal AKI postoperatively.
Poor walk test performance reflects frailty and limited physiologic reserve, which may correlate with complications including AKI, but is a nonspecific indirect marker.
Prior cardiovascular interventions suggest advanced vascular disease and higher procedural complexity, indirectly increasing AKI risk but without a direct mechanistic link.
Prior CABG can imply redo sternotomy/complex surgery and higher risk of hemodynamic instability, which can contribute to AKI, but remains an indirect predictor.
History of prior cardiac surgery is associated with increased operative complexity and potential for low-output states, indirectly raising AKI risk.
Prior PCI reflects coronary disease burden and possible prior contrast exposure history, but it is not a strong standalone predictor of postoperative creatinine ratio.
PCI during the same episode may imply recent contrast load and unstable coronary physiology, plausibly increasing AKI risk, though still indirect relative to direct renal markers.
Presence of a stent mainly indicates coronary disease history; it has limited direct physiologic linkage to postoperative renal injury beyond comorbidity burden.
May weakly proxy overall coronary disease burden and procedural complexity, which can correlate with perioperative instability. Not a direct renal perfusion or nephrotoxin marker.
History of prior interventions can indicate advanced cardiovascular disease and higher-risk redo physiology, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Still nonspecific for postoperative creatinine rise.

ACE inhibitors can reduce efferent arteriolar tone and predispose to perioperative hypotension/renal hypoperfusion, especially with CPB and vasoplegia. A recognized perioperative contributor to AKI risk in susceptible patients.
Primarily affects bleeding/transfusion risk rather than renal physiology directly. Any AKI association is indirect via hemodilution, hypotension, or transfusion-related effects.
Marker of arrhythmia/heart failure severity and potential hemodynamic instability, which can indirectly affect renal perfusion. Not a direct nephrotoxin in typical perioperative use.
Mostly relates to bleeding risk and perioperative management complexity; renal impact is indirect. Not a primary driver of postoperative creatinine rise.
Low-dose aspirin is generally not a major cause of AKI in this setting and is often standard-of-care. Any effect on creatinine is weak and confounded by indication.
Can influence perioperative hemodynamics (bradycardia/low cardiac output) but is commonly continued and not a strong independent AKI predictor. Mostly a marker of cardiac disease management.
Chronic beta-blocker therapy duration is unlikely to add prognostic information beyond beta-blocker use and underlying cardiac disease. Minimal direct renal relevance.
Chronic CCB therapy has limited direct linkage to postoperative AKI and is largely a background comorbidity-treatment marker. Weak incremental signal for creatinine trajectory.
Often indicates recent ACS/high-risk coronary status and increases bleeding/transfusion risk, which can contribute indirectly to AKI. Not a direct renal biomarker.
Pre-op inotrope requirement is a strong marker of low cardiac output/shock physiology and poor renal perfusion reserve. This is an established risk factor for postoperative AKI after cardiac surgery.
Statins/lipid-lowering therapy are common and not a direct driver of AKI; effects are small and inconsistent. More reflective of baseline cardiovascular risk management.
Primarily reflects angina management and coronary disease, with minimal direct renal perfusion implication. Weak and nonspecific for AKI prediction.
IV nitrates suggest active ischemia/heart failure and can contribute to hypotension, indirectly risking renal hypoperfusion. Still an indirect marker rather than a direct AKI predictor.
Broad antianginal therapy category is nonspecific and largely overlaps with coronary disease severity. Limited direct mechanistic link to postoperative creatinine rise.
Chronic steroid use can reflect frailty/inflammation and may worsen infection risk and glycemic control, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Not a direct renal perfusion marker in this setting.
Primarily affects perioperative bleeding/transfusion needs rather than renal physiology directly. Any AKI association is indirect and inconsistent.

Warfarin exposure mainly influences bleeding and reversal strategy; it is not a typical direct driver of postoperative AKI. Downstream effects (transfusion/hemodynamics) are better captured by ICU variables.
Similar to other anticoagulants, the main impact is bleeding risk and perioperative management rather than kidney perfusion. Weak and indirect link to creatinine rise.
Anticoagulant timing is not a direct AKI mechanism; any effect is mediated through bleeding/transfusion and instability. Therefore low standalone predictive value.

Statins may be modestly protective via anti-inflammatory effects, but evidence for strong AKI prediction post-cardiac surgery is mixed. More a marker of baseline cardiovascular disease management than renal insult.

Recent catheterization can imply contrast exposure and higher baseline cardiovascular complexity, both associated with postoperative AKI risk. This is a recognized preoperative risk factor though not definitive alone.
Known coronary anatomy/disease reflects atherosclerotic burden and potential low cardiac reserve, indirectly affecting renal perfusion. However it is a broad marker and not specific for AKI.

Greater coronary disease burden can correlate with worse cardiac function and perioperative hemodynamic instability, increasing AKI risk indirectly. Still not a direct biomarker and overlaps with other severity indicators.
Severe aortic valve disease can predispose to perioperative low output states and renal hypoperfusion. Predictive value is indirect and often mediated by postoperative hemodynamics.

Mitral disease may be associated with pulmonary hypertension/low forward flow and perioperative instability, indirectly affecting kidneys. Not a strong standalone predictor compared with direct hemodynamic and renal labs.

Tricuspid disease/right-sided failure can cause venous congestion and elevated renal venous pressure, a plausible contributor to AKI. Still indirect and typically captured better by postoperative CVP/volume status variables (if available).
Pulmonic valve disease is uncommon and usually not a primary determinant of postoperative renal injury in cardiac surgery. Weak and nonspecific link to creatinine rise.
Redo sternotomy/cardiac surgery increases operative complexity, bypass time, bleeding/transfusion, and risk of low output—all associated with AKI. A meaningful established risk factor preoperatively.
Without clear clinical definition, this is likely administrative/categorical and not a direct physiologic driver of AKI. Treat as weak evidence to avoid spurious inclusion.
Case cancellation is an operational variable and does not reflect renal physiology. Any association with AKI would be incidental rather than causal.

Conversion (e.g., minimally invasive to open) is a proxy for intraoperative complexity/instability and longer ischemic/CPB exposure, which can increase AKI risk. Indirect and heterogeneous, so kept low for sparsity.

Robotic approach mainly reflects surgical technique and case selection rather than a direct renal perfusion or nephrotoxic mechanism. Any AKI association is likely confounded by patient/procedure selection.

CABG is associated with CPB exposure, hemodilution, inflammation, and perioperative hypotension, all established contributors to postoperative AKI. Procedure type is informative but not a direct biomarker.
Valve surgery often entails longer CPB/cross-clamp times and higher risk of low cardiac output syndrome, both linked to renal hypoperfusion and AKI. Useful baseline procedural risk signal.
Additional cardiac procedures generally indicate greater operative complexity and physiologic stress, which can raise AKI risk. Broad category with variable mechanisms, so capped low.
AF ablation/maze adds operative time but has limited direct linkage to renal injury compared with hemodynamic/CPB factors. Mostly an indirect complexity marker.

Aortic procedures are strongly associated with prolonged CPB, hypothermic circulatory arrest, embolic risk, and major hemodynamic perturbations, all established AKI risk factors. Still not a direct renal biomarker, so kept at established-risk level.
Concurrent non-cardiac procedures may increase operative duration and inflammatory burden, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Heterogeneous and confounded, so low.
Appropriate prophylactic antibiotic choice is standard-of-care and not a specific predictor of renal injury in this context. Any effect on creatinine is indirect and typically minimal.
Timing of prophylactic antibiotics reflects process quality rather than renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity. Weak mechanistic link to postoperative creatinine changes.
Discontinuation orders are workflow/process measures and do not directly reflect renal physiology. Limited relevance to AKI prediction.
An extra dose may proxy longer operations or higher blood loss, indirectly associated with AKI risk. The antibiotic exposure itself is usually not a dominant nephrotoxic driver in prophylaxis dosing.

Lower nadir temperature can indicate deeper hypothermia/complex CPB strategies and potential circulatory arrest, which correlate with renal hypoperfusion risk. Indirect and procedure-dependent, so modest.

Low intraoperative hemoglobin reflects hemodilution/bleeding and reduced oxygen delivery, which can precipitate renal ischemia and AKI. Established perioperative risk factor for postoperative creatinine rise.
Intraoperative hyperglycemia is associated with stress response and worse outcomes, and may correlate with AKI risk via inflammation/endothelial dysfunction. Indirect and confounded, so kept low.

Use of coronary perfusion techniques is a marker of operative strategy/complexity and myocardial protection needs, indirectly related to postoperative low-output states that can affect kidneys. Not a direct renal predictor, so low.
Aortic cannulation is common and usually reflects standard CPB setup rather than renal-specific risk. Any AKI signal is largely mediated through CPB duration/complexity captured elsewhere.

Femoral cannulation can indicate more complex aortic pathology or emergent/redo cases with higher embolic and malperfusion risk, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Still a proxy for case complexity rather than a direct renal insult.

Axillary cannulation often reflects aortic disease/arch work and altered perfusion strategies, which can correlate with higher perioperative risk including AKI. The effect is indirect and overlaps with circulatory arrest/CPB time.

Innominate cannulation is typically used in arch/aortic procedures and may mark higher operative complexity and embolic risk. Association with AKI is indirect and likely mediated by arrest time and hemodynamics.
Other cannulation sites mainly serve as a marker of atypical anatomy or complex surgery. Renal impact is nonspecific and largely captured by bypass/arrest variables.
Femoral venous cannulation can indicate minimally invasive approaches or complex access issues; it may correlate with longer CPB or hemodynamic instability. The link to AKI is indirect.
Jugular venous cannulation is uncommon and usually reflects access strategy rather than renal physiology. Any AKI association is weak and confounded by procedure type.
Right atrial venous cannulation is standard for many CPB cases and does not specifically predict renal injury. Limited incremental information beyond CPB time and postoperative hemodynamics.
Left atrial cannulation is procedure-specific and may reflect complex intracardiac operations. Renal risk is indirect and typically mediated through CPB duration and perioperative instability.
Pulmonary venous cannulation is rare and not a recognized driver of AKI. Any association would be through overall surgical complexity rather than kidney perfusion directly.
Bicaval cannulation often accompanies more complex valve/congenital-type intracardiac work and may correlate with longer CPB. The renal link is indirect and modest.
Other venous cannulation sites primarily indicate atypical or complex operative setup. They are not direct renal injury markers and add limited independent AKI signal.

Longer CPB time is a well-established predictor of postoperative AKI via inflammation, hemolysis, nonpulsatile flow, and renal hypoperfusion. It is one of the strongest perioperative drivers of creatinine rise in cardiac surgery.

Use of circulatory arrest indicates major aortic/arch surgery with high risk of global hypoperfusion and ischemia-reperfusion injury, increasing AKI risk. It is a strong procedural risk factor even after accounting for CPB time.

Longer DHCA time directly reflects duration of profound low-flow/ischemic exposure and is strongly associated with postoperative AKI. It captures severity of systemic hypoperfusion beyond a binary arrest indicator.

Cerebral perfusion use is mainly a marker of arch surgery complexity and altered perfusion strategy; renal effects are indirect. It may correlate with longer arrest/CPB but is not a direct kidney biomarker.

Longer selective cerebral perfusion often reflects complex arch surgery and prolonged cardiopulmonary bypass, which can increase systemic inflammatory response and renal hypoperfusion risk. It is an indirect proxy rather than a kidney-specific driver.

Circulatory arrest duration is a strong marker of global ischemia and profound hemodynamic insult, which can precipitate ischemic ATN and postoperative AKI. Longer arrest times correlate with higher AKI risk in complex cardiac/arch procedures.

Aortic occlusion indicates a more complex operative course and potential embolic/ischemic risk, but it is not a direct renal biomarker. Its effect on creatinine is largely mediated through bypass time and hemodynamics.

Longer cross-clamp time is an established risk factor for AKI via prolonged ischemic time, CPB exposure, and postoperative low cardiac output. It is a common operative severity marker linked to renal injury risk.
Use of cerebral oximetry is primarily a monitoring/behavioral variable and does not directly affect renal perfusion. It may weakly proxy case complexity but adds limited kidney-specific signal.

Diffuse aortic calcification reflects systemic atherosclerosis and higher risk of embolic events and perioperative hemodynamic instability, which can contribute to AKI. The link is plausible but indirect.

Documentation of aortic assessment is largely procedural/standard-of-care and mainly reflects workflow rather than renal physiology. Any association with AKI would be indirect through case complexity.

Plaque assessment may indicate concern for atheroembolism and complex aortic manipulation, which can contribute to renal injury. However, it is a weak proxy compared with direct hemodynamic and renal markers.

An altered operative plan due to aortic condition suggests higher surgical complexity and potential for longer bypass/cross-clamp and embolic risk, which can increase AKI risk. Still, it is an indirect indicator and should remain low to avoid redundancy with operative times.
Refusal of blood products is not a physiologic marker of renal injury and is uncommon; any effect would be mediated by subsequent anemia/hypoperfusion. It is unlikely to add stable predictive value.

Receiving blood products reflects bleeding, hemodilution, and operative complexity, all associated with AKI risk via inflammation and hypoperfusion. Kept modest to avoid collinearity with specific component transfusions.

RBC transfusion is associated with AKI after cardiac surgery, likely reflecting bleeding severity, anemia/oxygen delivery issues, and transfusion-related inflammation. It is a more specific and established signal than the aggregate blood-products variable.
FFP transfusion indicates coagulopathy/bleeding and greater operative stress, which can correlate with AKI risk. The relationship is indirect and often overlaps with overall transfusion burden.

Platelet transfusion suggests significant bleeding or platelet dysfunction (often CPB-related), which can accompany hypotension and inflammation contributing to AKI. It is a weaker, indirect predictor compared with RBCs and operative ischemic times.
Cryoprecipitate use reflects severe bleeding/coagulopathy and operative complexity, which may increase AKI risk through shock and inflammation. It is relatively infrequent and indirect.

Intraoperative clotting factor administration is a marker of coagulopathy/bleeding severity and complex surgery, which can be associated with renal hypoperfusion. It is not a direct renal mechanism and overlaps with transfusion variables.
Antifibrinolytic use is primarily related to bleeding management and has no consistent direct link to postoperative AKI beyond general illness severity.
Tranexamic acid is used to reduce bleeding; it is not a direct driver of renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity in typical cardiac surgery dosing.
TEE performance reflects procedural monitoring and case complexity rather than a physiologic mechanism causing AKI.
Residual aortic regurgitation can contribute to low effective forward flow and congestion, indirectly worsening renal perfusion, but is not a direct biomarker of AKI.
Residual MR may lead to low cardiac output and elevated venous pressures, which can impair renal perfusion indirectly.
Residual TR is closely tied to right-sided congestion and elevated renal venous pressure, a recognized contributor to postoperative cardiorenal AKI.
Valve surgery indicates operative type/complexity and potential bypass time, but is an indirect proxy rather than a direct renal injury mechanism.
Concomitant AF procedures add operative complexity but have limited direct physiologic linkage to renal tubular injury compared with hemodynamics and perfusion variables.
General anesthesia is near-universal in cardiac surgery and provides little discriminatory information for AKI risk in this cohort.
PCC use suggests significant bleeding/coagulopathy and higher acuity, which can correlate with AKI risk, but it is not a direct renal insult itself.
Higher residual aortic gradients may reflect suboptimal valve hemodynamics and potential low forward flow, but the relationship to AKI is indirect.
Paravalvular leak can cause hemodynamic inefficiency and hemolysis-related stress, indirectly increasing AKI risk, though typically not a primary driver.
Residual mitral stenosis physiology can limit cardiac output and raise left atrial pressures, indirectly affecting renal perfusion.
Mitral PVL may contribute to hemodynamic instability and hemolysis, which can be associated with AKI, but evidence is indirect.
Combined residual AV valve regurgitation can worsen low output and venous congestion, both relevant pathways for postoperative AKI, but remains an indirect marker.
Lower post-procedure EF is an established risk factor for low cardiac output syndrome and renal hypoperfusion after cardiac surgery, increasing AKI risk.
Planned PCI is an indirect marker of coronary disease complexity and potential contrast exposure, but it is not a direct peri-ICU driver of postoperative AKI in this setting.
IABP use indicates low cardiac output/cardiogenic shock physiology and need for mechanical support, which strongly relates to renal hypoperfusion and AKI risk.
Baseline cath assist suggests higher preoperative acuity/complexity, but its relationship to creatinine rise is indirect compared with direct hemodynamic and renal markers.
ECMO reflects severe circulatory/respiratory failure with high risk of renal hypoperfusion, hemolysis/inflammation, and frequent need for RRT, making it a strong predictor of creatinine rise.
Blood product transfusion is associated with inflammation, hemolysis, and severity of bleeding/shock, all of which increase AKI risk after cardiac surgery.
Extubation in the OR is mainly a marker of uncomplicated immediate recovery; it has only an indirect association with later AKI risk.
Re-intubation signals postoperative instability (respiratory failure, shock, sepsis) that can worsen renal perfusion, but it is not kidney-specific.
ICU visit is routine after cardiac surgery and provides little discriminatory information for AKI risk in this cohort.
Low postoperative hemoglobin can reflect bleeding/hemodilution and reduced oxygen delivery, which can contribute to renal ischemia and AKI.
Postoperative hematocrit overlaps heavily with hemoglobin; it is a plausible marker of anemia/hemodilution but is capped to reduce collinearity.
Being intubated postoperatively is common and often protocol-driven; it is only an indirect marker of illness severity relevant to AKI.
Prior MI indicates underlying cardiac dysfunction/atherosclerosis that can predispose to low-output states, but it is an indirect predictor compared with perioperative hemodynamics.
Timing of prior MI has weak and inconsistent linkage to postoperative renal injury once current perioperative physiology is accounted for.
Symptom timing at admission is not mechanistically linked to renal perfusion or nephrotoxic exposure and is unlikely to add predictive value for AKI.
Symptom timing at surgery is not a direct driver of postoperative creatinine rise and is likely redundant with overall acuity captured elsewhere.
Angina class reflects coronary disease severity and potential perioperative ischemic risk, but its association with AKI is indirect and modest.

Higher NYHA class reflects worse baseline cardiac reserve and propensity to low cardiac output states that can reduce renal perfusion postoperatively. Indirect risk factor and partly collinear with heart failure/shock variables.
Cardiogenic shock implies severe renal hypoperfusion and high vasopressor/inotrope exposure, a strong driver of ischemic ATN and postoperative AKI. Highly prognostic for subsequent creatinine rise.
Need for resuscitation suggests hemodynamic instability (hypotension, bleeding, low flow) that can precipitate AKI. Signal is meaningful but heterogeneous and less specific than shock.
Arrhythmias can reduce effective cardiac output and renal perfusion, but this broad category is nonspecific and overlaps with more granular rhythm features. Thus only a small inclusion probability.

VT/VF episodes indicate severe instability and potential low-flow states with ischemic kidney injury risk. Important but typically episodic and mediated through hemodynamics already captured elsewhere.
Sick sinus syndrome mainly reflects conduction disease and pacing needs rather than direct renal injury mechanisms. Weak and indirect association with AKI.
Atrial flutter may contribute to variable cardiac output and anticoagulation needs, but is not a strong independent AKI driver. Likely collinear with overall arrhythmia/AF burden.
Second-degree AV block is primarily a conduction abnormality; renal impact is indirect unless causing sustained bradycardia/hypotension. Weak standalone predictor.
Complete heart block can cause significant bradycardia and low cardiac output until paced, which can threaten renal perfusion. Still generally treatable and less directly predictive than shock.
Being paced is more a marker of conduction disease/management than a direct renal insult. Limited independent prognostic value for creatinine rise.

Heart failure is an established AKI risk factor via reduced renal perfusion, venous congestion, and higher likelihood of low-output states after surgery. Moderate independent relevance in elderly cardiac surgery patients.

Atrial fibrillation can worsen hemodynamics and is associated with postoperative complications, but its AKI effect is mostly mediated through cardiac output/vasopressor needs. Kept modest to avoid redundancy with arrhythmia and shock/heart failure.
Preoperative pain score has no clear physiologic linkage to postoperative renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity. Likely reflects subjective symptoms rather than AKI risk.
Higher cell-saver volume can proxy surgical bleeding/complexity and transfusion exposure, which correlate with inflammation and AKI risk. Indirect and confounded, so low probability.
Heparin dosing before bypass is protocol-driven and not a direct mechanism for AKI. Any association is likely through case complexity and thus weak.
Protamine can rarely cause hypotension/pulmonary vasoconstriction, potentially affecting renal perfusion, but dosing is largely standardized. Overall weak-to-indirect predictor.

Viscoelastic testing (e.g., TEG/ROTEM) reflects coagulation management rather than renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity. Any association with AKI is indirect via bleeding/transfusion and is better captured by hemodynamics and fluid balance features.
Use of a volatile anesthetic is common and not a specific driver of postoperative AKI. Any renal effect is largely mediated through blood pressure/flow and bypass factors captured elsewhere.
Isoflurane choice is primarily an anesthetic practice pattern and has no strong direct link to postoperative creatinine rise. Potential effects are indirect via hemodynamics.

Sevoflurane has theoretical nephrotoxicity concerns (compound A) but clinically significant AKI signal in modern cardiac anesthesia is weak. Any effect is small compared with perfusion pressure, congestion, and nephrotoxins.
Desflurane selection is not an established predictor of AKI after cardiac surgery. Renal risk is better explained by perfusion and postoperative physiology.
Other volatile agents represent heterogeneous practice variation without a consistent mechanistic link to AKI. Likely redundant with overall anesthetic/hemodynamic variables.
Pre-bypass volatile use is unlikely to independently predict AKI beyond its effects on peri-induction blood pressure. Those hemodynamic effects are better captured by BP/vasopressor features.

During-bypass volatile use is mainly a marker of anesthetic technique and does not directly reflect renal ischemia risk. AKI risk on bypass is more tied to perfusion pressure/flow, hemolysis, and inflammation.
Post-bypass volatile use may correlate with hemodynamic instability but is not a direct renal biomarker. Any association is indirect and likely collinear with BP/pressors.
Maintenance volatile use is routine and not a strong independent predictor of creatinine rise. Renal outcomes are more driven by perfusion, congestion, and nephrotoxic exposures.

Dexmedetomidine can influence sympathetic tone and blood pressure, potentially affecting renal perfusion, but evidence for a strong AKI effect is inconsistent. Likely acts through hemodynamics already represented elsewhere.
Propofol infusion mainly reflects anesthetic strategy and can cause hypotension, but it is not an established independent AKI predictor. Hemodynamic variables are more proximal to kidney injury.
Midazolam infusion is not mechanistically linked to renal tubular injury and is largely a sedation practice marker. Any association with AKI would be weak and indirect.

Higher insulin requirement can proxy perioperative hyperglycemia/diabetes severity and stress response, which are associated with AKI risk. It remains an indirect marker and not a direct renal injury measure.

Baseline systolic BP reflects chronic hypertension/vascular disease and starting perfusion reserve, which can influence susceptibility to perioperative renal hypoperfusion. It is an established but nonspecific risk factor.

Baseline diastolic BP relates to vascular tone and coronary/renal perfusion pressure and may reflect chronic hypertension burden. It is a modest, indirect predictor compared with postoperative hemodynamics and renal labs.
Pre-induction heart rate is a nonspecific marker of stress/pain/anxiety and does not directly reflect renal perfusion or tubular injury risk in this setting.
Use of a PA catheter is mainly a proxy for higher acuity/complex hemodynamics rather than a causal AKI mechanism; may weakly correlate with postoperative renal risk.
Maximum core temperature intraoperatively is not a well-established driver of post-cardiac surgery AKI compared with perfusion pressure, congestion, and nephrotoxins.

Inhaled nitric oxide use reflects pulmonary hypertension/RV strain and higher operative complexity, which can indirectly increase AKI risk via low output/congestion, but it is not a direct renal biomarker.

Large crystalloid volumes can contribute to hemodilution and venous congestion, indirectly worsening renal function, but volume given is confounded by bleeding/vasoplegia and is not a direct AKI predictor.
Synthetic colloids (e.g., HES) have recognized nephrotoxic associations and can increase AKI risk, though use in cardiac surgery varies and is confounded by severity/bleeding.
Albumin use is generally not nephrotoxic and mostly reflects resuscitation strategy/illness severity; any AKI association is indirect via fluid balance and hemodynamics.
Intraoperative hypoglycemia/low glucose is not a typical direct driver of AKI; glycemic extremes more often relate to overall stress response and management complexity.
Intraoperative vasodilator use can indicate vasoplegia or attempts to manage afterload; hypotension/low perfusion risk is the relevant pathway but medication use itself is an indirect proxy.
Intra-procedural EEG monitoring reflects neurologic monitoring needs and operative complexity, with no direct physiologic linkage to renal injury.
Pre-incision TEE is a monitoring choice and proxy for case complexity rather than a direct determinant of postoperative creatinine rise.
Reduced preoperative LVEF is an established risk factor for postoperative low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion, increasing AKI risk after cardiac surgery.
RV dysfunction predisposes to elevated venous pressures and renal congestion as well as low forward flow, both important contributors to postoperative AKI.
Mitral regurgitation severity can correlate with heart failure physiology and perioperative hemodynamic instability, but its relationship to AKI is indirect and confounded.
Mitral stenosis may indicate chronic pulmonary hypertension and right-sided strain, but it is not a primary or direct predictor of postoperative AKI.
Aortic regurgitation can be associated with chronic volume overload and perioperative complexity, yet its link to AKI is indirect compared with direct perfusion/congestion measures.

Significant valvular disease can contribute to perioperative low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion, but its effect is indirect and often mediated by postoperative hemodynamics captured elsewhere.
This is primarily a documentation/assessment indicator rather than a physiologic variable and has no direct mechanistic link to AKI risk.

Moderate-severe tricuspid regurgitation can reflect right-sided failure and venous congestion, which can worsen renal function, but it is an indirect predictor compared with postoperative pressures/diuresis.
Presence of a PFO has minimal direct relationship to renal perfusion or nephrotoxic exposure and is unlikely to predict creatinine rise.
Assessment performed is a process-of-care marker and does not itself represent a physiologic insult or established AKI risk factor.
Aortic size is not a direct driver of postoperative renal injury; any association would be weak and confounded by procedure type and bypass time.
Greater aortic atheroma burden may increase embolic risk and overall vascular disease severity, which can indirectly relate to renal vulnerability, but it is not a primary AKI determinant.
Mobile atheroma increases risk of atheroembolism during manipulation/CPB, which can contribute to renal injury, though events are relatively uncommon and indirect.
This is largely a procedural/assessment indicator and does not directly encode renal perfusion, nephrotoxicity, or kidney injury.
Aortic arch atheroma severity reflects systemic atherosclerosis and potential embolic burden, which can indirectly increase AKI risk but is not a strong standalone predictor.
Mobile arch atheroma raises risk of cholesterol/atheroembolization during surgery, a plausible contributor to postoperative renal dysfunction, but still not definitive.
Use of cardiopulmonary bypass is an established AKI risk factor via inflammation, hemolysis, nonpulsatile flow, and hypotension; it meaningfully stratifies risk in cardiac surgery patients.
Autologous priming/retrograde autologous priming may modestly affect hemodilution and transfusion needs, but its relationship to AKI is indirect and inconsistent.
Higher crystalloid volumes can reflect hemodilution and fluid overload, which may worsen renal congestion and outcomes, but volume is a nonspecific proxy for case complexity.
Synthetic colloids (e.g., HES) have recognized nephrotoxicity and are associated with AKI in critical illness; intraoperative exposure can plausibly increase postoperative creatinine.
Albumin use is generally not directly nephrotoxic; it more often reflects volume management and case severity, making it a weak-to-indirect predictor of AKI.

Need for hemofiltration/perfusion is a direct marker of severe perioperative fluid overload and/or early renal dysfunction and is strongly associated with subsequent creatinine rise after cardiac surgery.
Difficulty weaning inotropes from CPB suggests low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion risk, but it is an indirect proxy and often transient immediately post-op.
High vasopressor requirement at CPB separation reflects vasoplegia/shock physiology that can reduce renal perfusion pressure and increase AKI risk, though it is still an early indirect marker.
Having a post-op TEE is largely procedural/monitoring practice and does not directly quantify renal perfusion or injury risk.
SAM can cause dynamic LVOT obstruction and hypotension, which could impair renal perfusion, but it is uncommon and typically rapidly recognized/treated.
Return-to-CPB/echo-related workflow is a nonspecific procedural marker and does not directly measure renal injury; any effect is mediated through other hemodynamic variables.
Reduced LVEF is an established risk factor for low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion, but a single intraoperative estimate is indirect and collinear with downstream hemodynamics.
Postoperative RV dysfunction can drive low forward flow, venous congestion, and elevated renal venous pressure, increasing AKI risk; still an indirect predictor without concurrent hemodynamic data.
Intraoperative arrhythmias can cause transient hypotension and reduced renal perfusion, but are often brief and treated, making the link to later creatinine rise modest.
Core temperature reflects rewarming/hypothermia management and is not a primary driver of AKI; associations are weak and largely confounded by illness severity.
Elevated INR may indicate hepatic hypoperfusion/CPB-related coagulopathy and overall severity, but it is not a direct renal biomarker and has an indirect relationship to AKI.
Leukocytosis is a nonspecific inflammatory/stress marker after surgery and has limited specificity for predicting renal injury.
Thrombocytopenia after CPB can reflect inflammation/consumption and severity of illness, which correlates with AKI risk, but it is indirect.
Low hematocrit/anemia can reduce oxygen delivery to renal tissue and is associated with transfusion/bleeding, but it is not a definitive predictor on its own.
Fibrinogen changes mainly reflect acute phase response or bleeding/coagulopathy and are not directly linked to renal tubular injury.
Elevated postoperative lactate is a strong marker of systemic hypoperfusion/shock and correlates with renal ischemic injury risk, making it a strong early predictor of later creatinine rise.

Postoperative propofol use is mostly a marker of ongoing sedation/ventilation needs and overall acuity rather than a direct renal insult. Any AKI link is indirect via hemodynamic effects and illness severity.
Pain score on POD3 is temporally distant and not a physiologic driver of renal injury. It is more reflective of comfort/analgesia practices than kidney perfusion or nephrotoxicity.

Active smoking is associated with vascular disease and perioperative pulmonary complications, which can indirectly worsen renal perfusion. However it is not a strong direct predictor of early postoperative creatinine rise compared with hemodynamics and renal labs.
Former smoking may correlate with comorbidity burden but has a weak and indirect relationship to postoperative AKI. It adds limited incremental signal beyond cardiovascular disease variables.

Atrial fibrillation can reflect underlying cardiac disease and predispose to perioperative low cardiac output episodes that reduce renal perfusion. Paroxysmal AF is a modest, indirect risk factor rather than a direct biomarker.

Persistent AF more strongly reflects chronic cardiac dysfunction and risk of perioperative hemodynamic instability, which can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Still, it is an established but nonspecific risk factor for AKI.
Antegrade cardioplegia delivery technique has no clear direct mechanistic link to renal tubular injury. Any association with AKI would be confounded by procedure type and bypass complexity.
Retrograde cardioplegia is primarily a surgical technique choice and not a direct determinant of renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity. Potential AKI association is indirect via operative complexity.

Blood cardioplegia type is not a direct renal risk factor; renal outcomes are more driven by perfusion pressure, bypass time, and postoperative hemodynamics. Any signal would likely be confounded by surgical practice patterns.
Crystalloid cardioplegia type has no established direct relationship to postoperative creatinine rise. Renal injury risk is better captured by hemodynamic and renal function variables.

Acute heart failure indicates perioperative low cardiac output and venous congestion risk, both of which can reduce renal perfusion and worsen AKI. It is an established clinical risk factor but not a direct biomarker.

Chronic heart failure is associated with baseline renal vulnerability, congestion, and reduced renal reserve, increasing AKI risk after cardiac surgery. It is a known comorbidity-level predictor with moderate specificity.

Systolic dysfunction predisposes to low forward flow and perioperative hypotension/vasopressor needs, which can precipitate renal hypoperfusion. This is an established risk factor for postoperative AKI.

Diastolic dysfunction can contribute to elevated filling pressures and renal venous congestion, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Compared with systolic failure, the link is less direct and often confounded by age and hypertension.

Early mean oxygen saturation in the immediate post-op window is often transiently affected by anesthesia/ventilation and is a weak proxy for systemic oxygen delivery. Per epoch rules, 0–12h features are scored conservatively and mean values are less informative than extremes.

Sustained lower oxygenation in 13–24h may reflect ongoing respiratory failure and reduced oxygen delivery, indirectly contributing to renal ischemic risk. As a mean (not an extreme) and a nonspecific marker, it remains a low-probability inclusion.
Sustained average oxygenation is only an indirect contributor to AKI risk (via overall illness severity) and is less informative than hypoxemic extremes.
High SpO2 is not a pathologic driver of AKI and mainly reflects adequate oxygen delivery or supplemental oxygen use.
Maximum SpO2 is not mechanistically linked to renal injury and is largely treatment-dependent.
Maximum SpO2 provides little AKI signal compared with hypoxemia or hemodynamic variables.
Hypoxemic episodes can worsen renal medullary hypoxia, but early post-op desaturations are common/transient; min is the most relevant aggregation.
Persistent or recurrent hypoxemia in the trajectory phase can contribute to renal ischemia and reflects ongoing cardiopulmonary instability; min captures the insult.
Late hypoxemia close to the outcome window is a stronger leading indicator of systemic hypoperfusion/respiratory failure that can precipitate AKI.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity and routine ICU care rather than a direct physiologic mechanism for AKI.
More frequent SpO2 documentation is a weak proxy for acuity and is not specific for impending AKI.
Increased monitoring may reflect clinical concern for instability, but it remains a nonspecific behavioral signal and should be kept low.
Average PAP relates indirectly to RV function/venous congestion, but early post-op values are often transient and mean is less informative than extremes.
Sustained pulmonary hypertension/RV strain can contribute to renal venous congestion and low forward flow, but mean is still a smoothed signal.
Later sustained elevation may indicate ongoing RV dysfunction and congestion affecting renal perfusion; capped to avoid collinearity with the max feature.
Acute spikes in PAP can signal RV failure or high PVR with reduced cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion, though early post-op instability is common (epoch cap applies).
Max PAP in this phase better captures clinically meaningful RV strain/venous congestion that can drive AKI via low forward flow and elevated renal venous pressure.
Late elevated PAP peaks are a strong leading indicator of persistent RV dysfunction/venous congestion and impaired renal perfusion close to the creatinine outcome.

Low pulmonary artery pressure can reflect low RV output/low preload states that may reduce renal perfusion, but early post-op values are often transient and treatment-related. As a derived/aggregated minimum of a mean signal, it is an indirect marker for AKI risk.

Persistently low PA pressures in the trajectory phase may indicate ongoing low flow or aggressive diuresis/vasodilation affecting renal perfusion. Still indirect and less specific than direct systemic perfusion measures.

Closer to the outcome window, low PA pressures may better capture sustained low-flow physiology that can precipitate AKI. Remains nonspecific and collinear with other hemodynamic indicators, so kept modest.

Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity (e.g., PA catheter presence/acuity) rather than a renal injury mechanism. Early post-op monitoring is routine in many cardiac surgery patients.
More frequent PA pressure documentation may indicate ongoing hemodynamic instability prompting closer surveillance, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy and capped low.
Continued frequent PA pressure measurements late may signal persistent shock/complex management associated with AKI. As a proxy for clinician concern rather than physiology, kept <0.05.
Frequency of continuous cardiac index recording reflects device use and acuity, not a direct determinant of renal injury. In the immediate post-op period this is commonly protocolized.
Ongoing frequent CI measurements may indicate persistent hemodynamic instability, which is associated with AKI risk. Still a monitoring-intensity proxy and therefore low.
Late continued intensive CI monitoring can mark unresolved low-output/shock physiology linked to AKI. As a measured-count feature (not the CI value), it remains capped below 0.05.

Elevated CVP can impair renal venous outflow and reduce GFR (congestive nephropathy), but early post-op CVP is often influenced by ventilation and fluid shifts. Mean CVP is less sensitive than extremes for congestion-related AKI.
Sustained venous congestion in this phase is a recognized contributor to AKI after cardiac surgery. Mean CVP captures ongoing congestion but is still less specific than peak CVP.

Persistently elevated CVP close to hour 60 strongly suggests ongoing venous congestion/right-sided dysfunction, a well-established AKI mechanism post-cardiac surgery. Mean is informative for sustained congestion when near the outcome window.

High peak CVP reflects acute venous congestion (often from RV dysfunction, tamponade physiology, or high intrathoracic pressures) that can directly worsen renal filtration. Early post-op transience limits the score per epoch cap.
A high CVP maximum during the trajectory phase suggests clinically meaningful congestion episodes associated with subsequent AKI. Extremes better capture pathologic congestion than mean values.

Late high peak CVP is a strong direct indicator of ongoing venous congestion/right heart failure, which is tightly linked to worsening creatinine after cardiac surgery. As an extreme close to the outcome, it is prioritized over mean/min to reduce collinearity.

Low CVP may reflect hypovolemia/vasodilation and potential renal hypoperfusion, but it is nonspecific and heavily confounded by ventilation and immediate post-op management. Given collinearity with other CVP summaries, kept low.

Low CVP can reflect relative hypovolemia/low preload and potentially reduced renal perfusion, but the minimum is less informative for AKI than sustained venous congestion (high CVP). Capped to limit redundancy with other CVP summaries.

In the closest window to outcome, a low CVP may indicate ongoing underfilling or aggressive diuresis with risk of renal hypoperfusion, but it is still an indirect marker compared with high CVP/venous congestion. Kept low to avoid collinearity with other hemodynamic indicators.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a renal mechanism. Early post-op CVP checks are routine after cardiac surgery.
More frequent CVP documentation may indicate hemodynamic instability, but it is a behavioral proxy and not a direct AKI driver. Kept <0.05 per rule.
Increased CVP measurement closer to hour 60 can signal clinician concern for shock/volume issues that relate to AKI risk, but remains an indirect proxy. Scored conservatively below 0.05.

Average FiO2 in the immediate post-op period largely reflects routine ventilator management and transient pulmonary issues, with weak direct linkage to AKI. Mean is also less specific than extremes for acute insults.
Persistently higher oxygen requirement may correlate with overall severity of illness and cardiopulmonary dysfunction, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Mean FiO2 is a nonspecific marker, so kept low.

Ongoing elevated FiO2 requirement in the leading-indicator window can reflect persistent shock/ARDS and systemic inflammation associated with AKI, but remains indirect. Mean is less informative than FiO2 extremes, so conservative.

High peak FiO2 early may indicate acute respiratory failure or hemodynamic instability, which can accompany renal hypoperfusion, but early derangements are often transient post-CPB. Scored conservatively under the 0_12h cap.

A high FiO2 peak during the trajectory phase suggests significant pulmonary dysfunction and higher illness severity, indirectly associated with AKI. Still nonspecific and capped to reduce redundancy with FiO2 mean/min.

High peak FiO2 close to the outcome can mark persistent severe cardiopulmonary failure and systemic stress that increases AKI risk. It is an indirect severity marker, so kept at the plausible-link range.
Minimum FiO2 mainly reflects weaning/ventilator strategy rather than a pathologic insult driving AKI. Early post-op values are heavily protocol-driven.
Lower minimum FiO2 generally indicates better pulmonary status and is not a direct AKI trigger. Provides limited incremental prognostic value beyond other severity markers.

Inability to reduce FiO2 (i.e., higher minimum) could indicate persistent respiratory failure and higher acuity, indirectly linked to AKI risk. Still nonspecific and kept low to avoid collinearity with FiO2 max/mean.
Frequency of FiO2 recording is routine in ventilated patients and reflects documentation/monitoring rather than renal physiology. Not a specific AKI predictor.
FiO2 measurement frequency is a behavioral proxy and typically protocolized in the ICU. Weak and nonspecific association with AKI risk.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects clinician concern/respiratory instability rather than a direct renal mechanism. May weakly proxy overall severity that can correlate with AKI risk.
Ventilator set rate is not a direct driver of renal injury and early post-op settings are often protocolized. Mean adds collinearity without capturing a specific AKI-triggering insult.
Average set respiratory rate is a weak, indirect marker of illness severity and ventilation strategy. Not a primary determinant of renal perfusion/AKI.

Higher set rates can reflect acidosis/hypercapnia management and greater physiologic stress, indirectly associated with AKI. In the immediate 0–12h window this is often transient and protocol-driven, so kept low.
Persistently high set rates in 13–24h may indicate ongoing metabolic acidosis/sepsis/low output states that can predispose to AKI. Still indirect and not kidney-specific.
Low set rates usually reflect sedation/weaning strategy rather than renal pathophysiology. Early post-op values are commonly transient and not predictive of creatinine rise.
Minimum set rate is largely a management choice and weakly related to renal outcomes. Provides little incremental AKI signal.
Measurement frequency is a behavioral proxy and not a physiologic mechanism for AKI. Early post-op ventilator documentation is routine.
Documentation frequency mainly reflects workflow/acuity and is not kidney-specific. Weak proxy at best, so kept very low.
More frequent ventilator setting documentation late may reflect instability and higher overall severity, which can correlate with AKI. Still indirect and behavioral, so capped low.
Set tidal volume is typically protocolized (lung-protective ventilation) and not directly linked to renal injury. Mean value adds redundancy without capturing a specific AKI insult.
Average tidal volume is a weak severity/strategy marker and not a primary AKI predictor. Collinear with max and generally management-driven.
Later sustained higher tidal volumes may reflect poor lung compliance/ARDS and systemic inflammation that can contribute to AKI risk. Still indirect and largely ventilator-strategy dependent.

Higher maximum tidal volume can indicate non–lung-protective ventilation or poor compliance, indirectly associated with inflammation and AKI. In 0–12h this is often transient and protocol-related, so conservative.
A higher max tidal volume in 13–24h may signal ongoing respiratory failure/poor compliance and systemic stress that can track with AKI. Indirect association only.

In the 25–36h leading window, higher max tidal volume may reflect persistent respiratory failure and systemic inflammation/vasopressor needs that correlate with AKI. Still not kidney-specific, so kept below 0.05.
Low set tidal volume is not a direct AKI driver; it mainly reflects ventilator strategy. Early post-op settings are often protocolized and transient, limiting prognostic value for creatinine rise.
Minimum set tidal volume in this window is unlikely to causally affect renal perfusion and is typically guided by lung-protective protocols. Weak indirect link via severity of illness only.
Persistently low tidal volume may indicate ongoing respiratory failure/ARDS and higher illness severity, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still an indirect marker rather than a renal mechanism.
Measurement frequency reflects documentation/monitoring intensity rather than physiology. In the immediate post-op period it is routine and not specific for impending AKI.
How often tidal volume is recorded is a behavioral proxy and usually standard ICU practice. Not a specific predictor of renal injury.
More frequent ventilator setting documentation may reflect instability, but remains nonspecific and highly practice-dependent. Weak indirect association with AKI via acuity.

Higher mean PEEP can reduce venous return and renal perfusion, but early post-cardiac surgery PEEP is commonly standardized and transient. Mean is also less sensitive than extremes for acute hemodynamic insult.
Sustained moderate PEEP may contribute to lower cardiac output/renal perfusion and reflects ongoing pulmonary dysfunction. Still indirect and collinear with PEEP extremes, so kept low.

Persistently elevated mean PEEP closer to outcome can signal ongoing respiratory failure and potential hemodynamic compromise affecting kidney perfusion. Mean is less informative than max for acute insult, so capped at plausible-indirect.

PEEP spikes can acutely reduce preload/cardiac output and renal blood flow, plausibly contributing to AKI risk. In the immediate 0–12h period, derangements are common and transient, so conservatively scored.

Higher maximum PEEP in this trajectory phase suggests more severe lung disease and greater risk of hemodynamic compromise impacting renal perfusion. Extremes better capture insult than mean/min, so prioritized.

High PEEP maxima in the 25–36h leading window can directly worsen venous return and renal perfusion and indicate severe respiratory failure, both linked to AKI. This extreme is the most pathophysiologically relevant PEEP summary here.
Minimum PEEP largely reflects baseline ventilator settings and is not a trigger for AKI. Also redundant given max/mean PEEP summaries.
Low minimum PEEP is not mechanistically linked to renal injury and is typically protocol-driven. Provides little incremental information beyond max PEEP.
Minimum PEEP in this window is weakly informative at best and mainly reflects ventilator strategy rather than renal perfusion. Kept low to avoid collinearity with more relevant PEEP max.
Frequency of PEEP documentation is a monitoring/behavioral signal and is routine immediately post-op. Not specific for AKI risk and should remain very low.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects clinician monitoring intensity rather than a direct renal perfusion mechanism. Cap low to avoid using behavioral proxies for AKI risk.
More frequent PEEP documentation may indicate ongoing respiratory instability/ARDS, which can correlate with shock and AKI risk, but it is still an indirect behavioral marker. Keep <0.05 per rules.

Early post-op ventilator pressures are often transient and influenced by anesthesia/atelectasis; mean values smooth short insults. Plateau pressure is at best an indirect AKI correlate via severity of illness/hemodynamics.

Sustained higher plateau pressures can reflect worse lung mechanics and higher intrathoracic pressures affecting venous return/renal perfusion, but mean is less informative than extremes. Keep low to reduce collinearity with max/min.

High peak plateau pressures can acutely increase intrathoracic pressure and reduce cardiac output/renal perfusion, serving as a marker of severe respiratory mechanics. Conservative scoring due to 0–12h epoch cap and common transient derangements.

Persistently high maximum plateau pressure in the trajectory phase suggests ongoing poor compliance/ARDS and higher intrathoracic pressures that can worsen renal perfusion and correlate with multi-organ dysfunction. Prefer max over mean/min to capture acute insult.

Low minimum plateau pressure is not a recognized trigger for AKI and is generally reassuring/reflects ventilator settings rather than pathology. Also redundant given max better captures harmful physiology.
Minimum plateau pressure provides little signal for renal injury risk and is largely determined by ventilator strategy. Keep minimal to avoid collinearity with more relevant max.
Frequency of plateau pressure measurement reflects monitoring practices and ventilator management rather than kidney injury mechanisms. Per rules, keep very low.
More frequent plateau checks may indicate difficult ventilation or worsening respiratory status, indirectly associated with AKI via severity of illness. Still a behavioral proxy, so keep <0.05.

In the closest window to outcome, frequent plateau measurements may signal ongoing ventilatory instability and higher illness acuity that correlates with AKI risk. Nonetheless it is not a physiologic biomarker, so remain <0.05.

Minute ventilation is influenced by sedation, temperature, and ventilator settings immediately post-op; mean values dilute short episodes of acidosis/instability. Only an indirect marker of severity of illness for AKI.

Higher sustained minute ventilation can reflect metabolic acidosis/sepsis or increased dead space, which may co-occur with AKI, but it is nonspecific. Mean is less informative than extremes for capturing acute deterioration.

High maximum minute ventilation may indicate acute metabolic acidosis or respiratory distress, which can accompany hypoperfusion states linked to AKI. Conservative due to early epoch and indirect relationship.

A high maximum minute ventilation during 13–24h may signal evolving metabolic acidosis/shock physiology or worsening lung disease, both associated with AKI risk. Prefer max over mean/min to capture clinically meaningful extremes while limiting redundancy.
Low minimum minute ventilation is commonly due to sedation/ventilator weaning and is not a direct AKI risk factor. Also less informative than max for capturing pathologic stress.

Low minute ventilation can reflect hypoventilation/sedation or ventilator weaning rather than renal physiology; only indirectly relates to AKI via severity of illness and hypercapnia/hemodynamics. Using the minimum is the most plausible signal among MV aggregates, but still weak for kidney prediction in this epoch.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity and routine post-op ventilation management, not a causal AKI mechanism. Early (0–12h) ordering is common and nonspecific.
More frequent MV documentation may proxy respiratory instability/acuity, which can correlate with multi-organ dysfunction risk. Still a behavioral/monitoring feature and should remain low.

Persistent need for frequent ventilator assessments late (25–36h) can indicate ongoing critical illness and higher AKI risk via shock/inflammation. Nonetheless it is a monitoring proxy, not a direct renal predictor.

Average oxygen device level in the immediate post-op window is heavily driven by routine extubation/ICU protocols and is not kidney-specific. Mean further dilutes acute hypoxemic episodes relevant to AKI.

Higher average oxygen support may reflect ongoing cardiopulmonary dysfunction and illness severity, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Mean aggregation is less informative than extremes for acute insults.

Sustained higher oxygen device requirement closer to hour 60 can indicate persistent pulmonary edema/low cardiac output states associated with renal hypoperfusion. Still indirect and not a renal biomarker.

Maximum oxygen device requirement captures acute hypoxemia/respiratory failure, which can accompany hemodynamic compromise and raise AKI risk. In 0–12h this is often transient and protocol-driven, so kept conservative.

A high maximum oxygen device requirement during 13–24h suggests clinically significant respiratory deterioration and higher overall organ failure risk, including AKI. Max is preferred over mean/min to reduce collinearity and capture acute insult.

Late high oxygen device requirement (max) is a stronger marker of persistent cardiopulmonary failure/volume overload, which commonly coexists with renal hypoperfusion and AKI after cardiac surgery. Max best captures the pathologic extreme compared with mean/min.
Minimum oxygen device level largely reflects periods of stability or weaning and is not mechanistically linked to AKI. In the immediate post-op period it is especially protocol-dependent.
Minimum oxygen support indicates best respiratory status rather than insult severity and adds little incremental AKI signal beyond max/mean. Kept very low to avoid redundancy.
Minimum oxygen device requirement reflects transient improvement and is not a driver of renal injury. It is likely collinear with other oxygen-support summaries and not the pathologic direction.
Frequency of documenting oxygen device is a care-process variable and common immediately post-op. It does not directly represent renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity.
More frequent oxygen device assessments may indicate respiratory instability and higher acuity, indirectly associated with AKI. Still a behavioral proxy and should remain <0.05.

Late frequent oxygen device documentation can reflect ongoing respiratory failure/ICU intensity, which correlates with multi-organ dysfunction risk including AKI. As a measurement-frequency feature it remains low by design.

Average chest tube output is an indirect marker of bleeding/transfusion needs that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion, but early post-op output is often transient. Mean is less informative than peak bleeding for AKI risk.

Persistently higher average drainage may reflect ongoing bleeding/inflammation with potential hemodynamic impact, but remains an indirect AKI predictor. Mean is capped to reduce redundancy with max output.

Later sustained drainage can indicate ongoing bleeding or coagulopathy that may drive hypotension/transfusion and renal stress, but it is still indirect. Mean is kept low to avoid collinearity with max output.

Peak early bleeding can precipitate hypotension, transfusion, and low renal perfusion, increasing AKI risk. In the immediate 0–12h window, derangements are common, so the score is conservative and capped by epoch.

A high maximum output in 13–24h suggests ongoing bleeding/tamponade physiology risk and greater likelihood of hemodynamic instability affecting kidneys. Max better captures acute insult than mean/min.

High peak drainage close to the outcome window can signal clinically significant ongoing bleeding or re-exploration risk with renal hypoperfusion and transfusion exposure. This extreme value is the most pathophysiologically relevant aggregation.
Minimum chest tube output is not a reliable AKI mechanism and may reflect measurement artifacts or periods without drainage. It is less clinically meaningful than high output extremes.
Low minimum output does not directly indicate renal risk and is confounded by tube patency/position and documentation. Not a useful predictor compared with max output.
Minimum output late post-op is weakly related to AKI and may reflect intermittent recording rather than physiology. Kept minimal for sparsity.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects nursing/clinical attention and routine post-op monitoring rather than a causal AKI pathway. Early post-op measurement is standard and poorly discriminative.
More frequent checks may indicate concern for bleeding or instability, indirectly associated with AKI risk, but it is a behavioral proxy. Kept <0.05 per rule.
Increased measurement frequency late may reflect escalating concern for bleeding/tamponade and higher acuity, which can correlate with AKI. Still a proxy variable and capped <0.05.

Mean systolic PAP reflects RV afterload and pulmonary hypertension, which can contribute to low cardiac output and renal congestion, but early post-op values are often transient. Mean is less sensitive than extremes for acute decompensation.
Sustained elevated PAP in the trajectory phase can indicate persistent RV strain and venous congestion that may impair renal perfusion. Mean is capped to limit collinearity with max PAP.

Persistently high PAP closer to hour 60 suggests ongoing RV dysfunction/venous congestion, a recognized contributor to cardiorenal syndrome and AKI. Mean is moderately informative but less than extreme decompensation.

Peak PAP elevations can signal acute RV failure or pulmonary hypertensive crises leading to low forward flow and renal hypoperfusion. In 0–12h this may be transient post-bypass, so the score is conservative and epoch-capped.

Elevated PAP systolic can reflect RV strain/pulmonary hypertension and venous congestion, indirectly affecting renal perfusion. Use max (not mean) to capture episodic RV afterload spikes; capped to limit collinearity with other PAP summaries.

Late (25–36h) high PAP systolic suggests persistent RV afterload/strain and potential systemic venous congestion, which can worsen renal function. Max captures acute hemodynamic stress more than averages.

Low PAP systolic early post-op may reflect low flow states or measurement/ventilation effects, with only indirect linkage to AKI. Conservative scoring due to transient immediate post-bypass physiology (0–12h cap).

Low PAP systolic in the trajectory phase may indicate low cardiac output or reduced RV output, indirectly risking renal hypoperfusion. Still nonspecific and capped to avoid redundancy with other hemodynamic indicators.
Persistently low PAP systolic closer to outcome can signal ongoing low-flow physiology and reduced forward output, which can contribute to AKI. Min is the relevant extreme for hypoperfusion risk.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a renal mechanism. Early post-op monitoring is routine after cardiac surgery, limiting prognostic specificity.
More frequent PAP measurements may indicate clinician concern for hemodynamic instability, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy and kept <0.05 per rule.
Increased late measurement frequency can reflect ongoing shock/RV failure concern, which correlates with AKI risk. Remains a proxy variable and therefore kept low.

Mean PAP diastolic early is heavily influenced by ventilation/PEEP and immediate post-bypass changes, with only indirect renal relevance. Mean is less informative than extremes for acute insults and is down-weighted.

Sustained elevation in PAP diastolic can suggest pulmonary vascular load and RV dysfunction, indirectly contributing to venous congestion and AKI. Mean is still less specific than max/min, so kept low.
Later sustained PAP diastolic elevation may reflect persistent RV afterload and congestion physiology relevant to renal dysfunction. Mean is secondary to extremes, so importance remains modest.

Early spikes in PAP diastolic can occur with hypoxia/hypercarbia/PEEP and may transiently stress the RV, indirectly affecting renal perfusion. Conservative due to 0–12h transient physiology and epoch cap.

Max PAP diastolic in 13–24h can capture episodic pulmonary hypertensive crises/RV strain, which may precede renal congestion or low output. Capped to reduce collinearity with other PAP-derived features.

Late high PAP diastolic peaks suggest ongoing pulmonary hypertension/RV dysfunction and venous congestion, both linked to AKI after cardiac surgery. Max best captures clinically meaningful decompensation events.

Low PAP diastolic early is nonspecific and may reflect low preload/vasodilation or measurement variability, with weak direct linkage to AKI. Min is less clearly tied to renal outcomes than systemic MAP extremes.
Low PAP diastolic in 13–24h may indicate low pulmonary blood flow/low RV output, indirectly associated with renal hypoperfusion. Still nonspecific and kept low to maintain sparsity.

Low diastolic PA pressure can reflect low RV preload/forward flow or vasodilation with reduced effective perfusion pressure, which can contribute to renal hypoperfusion in the closest pre-outcome window. Use the minimum (extreme) as the most relevant aggregation for acute hemodynamic insult.

Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a renal mechanism, and early post-op monitoring is routine. Capped low per the _measured rule and conservative 0–12h window.
Ordering/recording frequency is a behavioral proxy and is not a direct driver of AKI risk. Keep low to avoid over-weighting surveillance patterns.
More frequent PA pressure documentation may indicate hemodynamic instability prompting concern for organ perfusion, but remains indirect. Kept <0.05 per _measured rule.

Average heart rate in the immediate post-op period is often transiently elevated from pain, catecholamines, and temperature, with weak specificity for AKI. Mean is also less informative than extremes for acute perfusion insults.
Sustained tachycardia can reflect ongoing stress/low stroke volume and may correlate with worse perfusion, but is nonspecific. Mean is capped low to reduce collinearity with pulse extremes.

Persistently abnormal heart rate closer to hour 60 can signal ongoing shock, arrhythmia burden, or high vasopressor needs that may impair renal perfusion. Still nonspecific, and mean is less informative than min/max.

Extreme tachycardia early may indicate bleeding, pain, or low cardiac output, which can contribute to renal hypoperfusion, but early post-op spikes are common and transient. Max is preferred over mean for capturing acute insult.

Recurrent/high peak tachycardia during the trajectory phase can reflect ongoing hemodynamic stress (e.g., low output, sepsis, AF with RVR) associated with AKI risk. Max captures clinically meaningful extremes better than mean.

High peak heart rates in the 25–36h window can indicate persistent instability or arrhythmias that reduce effective renal perfusion and increase AKI risk. Max is the most relevant aggregation for acute physiologic stress.

Bradycardia early can reduce cardiac output and renal blood flow, but is often transient and influenced by sedation/pacing post-cardiac surgery. Min is more relevant than mean for capturing low-output episodes.

Low minimum heart rate in this phase may reflect conduction issues, excessive beta-blockade, or pacing dependence with potential low cardiac output, which can worsen renal perfusion. Min captures the potentially harmful extreme.

Bradycardic episodes closest to the outcome can directly reduce renal perfusion via low cardiac output, especially in elderly post-cardiac surgery patients. Minimum heart rate is the most pathophysiologically relevant aggregation here.
Pulse measurement frequency is routine in the ICU and reflects monitoring intensity rather than kidney injury physiology. Kept at minimal importance per _measured rule.
More frequent pulse recordings are largely a function of nursing/monitoring protocols and acuity, not a direct AKI mechanism. Maintain very low inclusion probability to preserve sparsity.
Increased measurement frequency may weakly proxy instability in the lead-up window, but remains indirect and prone to practice-pattern confounding. Kept <0.05 per _measured rule.

Mean inspired O2/anesthetic gas exposure in the immediate post-op window is not a direct driver of renal injury and is largely routine ventilator/anesthesia management. Any association with AKI would be indirect via illness severity and is likely collinear with hemodynamics/oxygenation variables.

High O2 delivery may reflect transient respiratory instability or high FiO2 needs, which can correlate with overall acuity but is not a primary AKI mechanism. In 0–12h, this is often expected post-op and should be weighted conservatively.
Lower O2/anesthetic gas setting is typically a weaning/management choice rather than a renal perfusion insult. It has no established direct link to subsequent creatinine rise.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity and workflow rather than kidney physiology. In early post-op care this is routine and not specific for impending AKI.

More frequent documentation/measurement may weakly proxy ongoing respiratory instability or clinician concern, which can correlate with multi-organ dysfunction. Still nonspecific for AKI and capped low as a behavioral feature.

Persistently frequent measurement closer to the outcome may indicate continued critical illness and higher risk of organ dysfunction, including AKI. Nonetheless it remains an indirect behavioral proxy and should stay <0.05.

Nitrous oxide exposure has no meaningful established relationship with postoperative AKI compared with hemodynamics, nephrotoxins, and renal labs. Likely reflects anesthesia practice rather than renal risk.
