Markedly prolonged PTT closer to outcome can signal ongoing coagulopathy/anticoagulation with bleeding, transfusion, and hemodynamic instability that can precipitate AKI. Still an indirect predictor rather than a renal biomarker.
Low/normal PTT is not a plausible driver of AKI and is usually clinically uninformative for renal outcomes. Early values are heavily influenced by perioperative management.
Lower PTT does not directly indicate renal hypoperfusion or tubular injury and is unlikely to predict creatinine rise. Provides little AKI-specific signal.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity after surgery rather than a mechanism for AKI. Early post-op PTT checks are routine in many cardiac surgery pathways.
More frequent PTT checks may reflect concern for bleeding/heparin titration and higher acuity, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy and kept <0.05.

Repeated PTT monitoring late suggests ongoing anticoagulation management or coagulopathy/bleeding concern, which can accompany shock/transfusion and AKI. It remains an indirect, practice-pattern feature.
Monocyte count is a nonspecific inflammatory marker and not an established early predictor of post-cardiac surgery AKI. Mean values in the immediate post-op window are noisy and nonspecific.
Sustained inflammation may correlate with complications that increase AKI risk, but monocyte count is weak and nonspecific. Mean aggregation further dilutes any acute signal.
A high monocyte peak may reflect systemic inflammatory response after CPB, which is common and only indirectly related to AKI. Early epoch limits strength and specificity.
A higher inflammatory peak during the trajectory phase could indicate evolving infection/SIRS that can contribute to AKI risk. Still indirect and not a renal-specific marker.
Low monocyte counts are not a recognized driver of AKI in this context and are often clinically nonspecific. Adds little prognostic value for creatinine ratio.
Lower monocyte nadir is not mechanistically linked to renal hypoperfusion or tubular injury and is unlikely to predict AKI. Minimal expected signal.
CBC differential measurement frequency is largely routine post-op care and reflects monitoring rather than kidney injury mechanisms. As a behavioral proxy it is kept very low.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects clinician concern/inflammation workup rather than a direct AKI mechanism. Keep low to avoid behavioral-proxy overfitting.
More frequent CBC differentials late may signal evolving sepsis/instability that can contribute to AKI, but it remains an indirect behavioral marker. Capped <0.05 per rules.
Phosphorus can rise with reduced GFR, but early post-op values are often transiently affected by shifts/hemolysis and treatment. Mean is less sensitive than extremes for early renal signal.
Sustained phosphorus elevation can reflect impaired renal clearance during the trajectory phase, but mean is still less specific than a peak. Capped to limit collinearity with phosphorus_max.
Later sustained hyperphosphatemia may indicate evolving renal dysfunction, but the peak value better captures the pathologic insult. Capped at 0.05 to prioritize the extreme aggregation.
An early phosphorus spike can be an early biochemical signal of reduced renal clearance or major tissue injury, though immediate post-CPB derangements are common. Conservative due to 0–12h cap.
A higher phosphorus peak in 13–24h more plausibly reflects impaired renal excretion as the patient should be stabilizing. Moderate direct biomarker signal for evolving AKI.
Late hyperphosphatemia is a relatively direct marker of reduced GFR and correlates with worsening renal function approaching the outcome window. Strong leading-indicator biomarker for AKI risk.
Low phosphorus is more related to dilution, insulin/feeding shifts, or repletion practices than AKI pathophysiology. Minimal direct link to creatinine rise.
Phosphorus nadir is not a typical driver or marker of AKI compared with elevations. Likely reflects treatment/dilution rather than renal injury.
A low minimum phosphorus late is not a recognized predictor of AKI and is often iatrogenic or nutritional. Keep very low to avoid redundancy with more informative maxima.
Ordering frequency reflects monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. Early post-op labs are routine and not specific for impending AKI.
More frequent phosphorus checks may indicate concern for renal dysfunction or electrolyte derangements, but remains an indirect proxy. Kept <0.05 per measurement-frequency rule.
Late increased monitoring can reflect clinician suspicion of AKI/electrolyte issues, modestly informative but not mechanistic. Still capped below 0.05.
Neutrophilia early is common after surgery/CPB and is nonspecific; inflammation can contribute to AKI but this is indirect. Mean value is less informative than hemodynamics/renal labs.
Persistent neutrophilia may indicate ongoing inflammation/infection that can precipitate AKI via sepsis/hemodynamic effects, but it is not a direct renal marker. Capped low to maintain sparsity.

Post-op neutrophilia reflects inflammatory stress/infection risk, which can contribute indirectly to AKI, but is nonspecific in the immediate post-CPB period. Cap to avoid redundancy with other ANC summaries.
Persistently high ANC may indicate evolving infection/SIRS that can worsen renal perfusion and AKI risk, but remains an indirect marker. Kept low to limit collinearity with other ANC features.
Low ANC is not a typical driver or early biomarker of post-cardiac surgery AKI and is often unrelated to renal perfusion. Immediate post-op values are frequently transient and treatment-related.
Neutropenia is uncommon and not an established predictor of AKI in this context; any association would be indirect (e.g., severe illness). Prioritize other physiologic/hemodynamic predictors.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects routine post-op lab protocols rather than renal pathophysiology. In the first 12 hours it is especially protocol-driven.
More frequent ANC checks may reflect clinician concern for infection/instability, which can correlate with AKI risk, but it is a behavioral proxy. Kept <0.05 per rule.
Increased measurement frequency closer to outcome may indicate escalating concern for infection/SIRS and overall acuity, indirectly associated with AKI. Still a proxy variable, so kept low.
Troponin ordering frequency is largely routine after cardiac surgery and does not directly measure renal injury. Early post-op ordering is protocolized.
Repeated troponin checks may signal concern for perioperative MI/low cardiac output, which can reduce renal perfusion, but this is indirect and behavior-driven. Kept <0.05.
Ongoing troponin monitoring late may reflect suspected myocardial injury/low output state, a plausible contributor to AKI via hypoperfusion. Still a proxy for clinician concern, so low.

Average oxygenation in the immediate post-op window is influenced by ventilation strategy and transient atelectasis; hypoxemia can contribute to organ stress but is not a primary AKI driver. Mean is less informative than extremes for acute insults.
Sustained lower oxygenation may reflect ongoing respiratory failure and higher illness severity, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Mean values smooth extremes and are nonspecific.
Persistently impaired oxygenation closer to hour 60 can indicate ongoing critical illness and potential renal hypoxia risk, but remains indirect. Mean is kept modest versus extreme measures.
High PaO2 peaks usually reflect high FiO2 rather than pathology and are not established predictors of AKI. Early post-op hyperoxia is common and transient.
PaO2 maxima are largely driven by ventilator/FiO2 settings and do not capture renal hypoperfusion risk well. Not a meaningful AKI predictor.

Late PaO2 peaks remain primarily treatment-dependent and provide little specific information about renal injury risk. Prioritize hemodynamics, creatinine/urine output, and nephrotoxin exposure instead.

Low PaO2 can reflect hypoxemia and overall cardiopulmonary instability that may contribute to renal hypoperfusion, but early post-op derangements are often transient. As an indirect marker (and early epoch), keep conservative.
Persistent hypoxemia into 13–24h suggests ongoing respiratory/circulatory failure and higher AKI risk via reduced oxygen delivery. Still indirect and often confounded by ventilator strategy.
Hypoxemia closest to the outcome window can indicate sustained low oxygen delivery and severity of illness, which correlates with AKI risk. Not kidney-specific, so moderate weight only.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity and clinician concern rather than a causal physiologic driver of AKI. Early post-op ABG frequency is routine after cardiac surgery.
More frequent PaO2 checks may indicate instability, but it is a behavioral proxy and highly practice-dependent. Keep below 0.05 per rule.
Frequent ABGs late (25–36h) can signal ongoing critical illness, which correlates with AKI risk, but remains non-mechanistic and practice-driven. Capped <0.05.
Mean potassium early post-op is influenced by shifts, transfusion, and treatment; it is not a direct AKI marker in this window. Prefer extremes over mean to reduce collinearity.
Sustained potassium elevation can reflect reduced renal excretion, but mean blunts acute hyperkalemia and is treatment-confounded. Keep low and defer to max as the more pathologic aggregation.
Later sustained potassium elevation may reflect evolving renal dysfunction, but mean remains less specific than peak hyperkalemia. Capped at 0.05 to avoid redundancy with potassium_max.
Peak hyperkalemia can be an early signal of impaired renal excretion or severe tissue injury/hemolysis, both associated with AKI risk. Early epoch cap keeps it conservative.
Hyperkalemia in 13–24h is more suggestive of evolving renal dysfunction and correlates with subsequent creatinine rise. Peak value captures the clinically meaningful insult better than mean/min.
Peak hyperkalemia close to hour 60 is a strong direct indicator of reduced renal clearance and impending/worsening AKI. This is one of the most kidney-relevant signals among provided features.
Hypokalemia is usually iatrogenic (diuresis/insulin) and not a typical driver or marker of AKI in this context. Early post-op electrolyte swings are common and nonspecific.
Low potassium more often reflects treatment effects and does not directly indicate renal injury. Limited mechanistic link to creatinine rise.
Later hypokalemia remains more consistent with diuretic/insulin effects than AKI physiology. Weak and nonspecific for predicting creatinine_ratio.
Testing frequency reflects monitoring intensity and perceived instability rather than a direct renal mechanism. Early post-cardiac surgery electrolyte checks are routine, so keep low.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects clinician concern/acuity rather than a causal AKI mechanism. Some association is plausible because rising creatinine/oliguria prompts electrolyte monitoring.
Closer to the outcome window, frequent potassium checks can proxy evolving renal dysfunction or aggressive diuresis/renal replacement consideration. Still a behavioral marker, so kept <0.05.

Early postoperative bilirubin reflects hepatic congestion/hemolysis/CPB effects and overall illness severity, which can correlate with AKI risk. Mean is less informative than extremes for acute hypoperfusion-related injury.

An early bilirubin spike can indicate hemolysis, low-flow states, or hepatic congestion after CPB, which are associated with higher AKI risk. In the 0–12h epoch, derangements are often transient, so capped conservatively.
Low/normal bilirubin is not a meaningful driver or marker of AKI risk in this context. Provides little incremental information versus max/mean.
Ordering frequency is largely routine post-cardiac surgery and reflects practice patterns more than renal pathophysiology. Weak proxy for acuity only.
Repeat bilirubin checks may reflect concern for hepatic dysfunction/hemolysis, but this is an indirect and nonspecific signal for AKI. Kept very low to avoid over-weighting clinician behavior.
Later repeated bilirubin testing can indicate ongoing multi-organ dysfunction, which correlates with AKI risk. Still primarily a behavioral proxy, so kept <0.05.
Anemia/hemodilution can reduce oxygen delivery and correlate with transfusion needs, indirectly affecting AKI risk. Mean in the immediate post-op window is less specific than nadir.
Sustained anemia in the trajectory phase may reflect bleeding/hemodilution and ongoing transfusion exposure, both linked to AKI risk. Mean remains an indirect marker compared with minimum.

Persistently low RBC count closer to hour 60 may indicate ongoing anemia/bleeding and reduced oxygen delivery, which can worsen renal ischemia. Still indirect and potentially collinear with nadir measures.
Higher RBC count is not a typical trigger for AKI and often reflects hemoconcentration rather than renal injury. Adds little beyond other hemodynamic/volume markers.
RBC maxima are not a recognized AKI driver in this setting and are less clinically meaningful than anemia/nadir. Likely redundant with other volume status indicators.
Elevated RBC count/hemoconcentration is a nonspecific finding and not a direct AKI predictor compared with hypotension, oliguria, or creatinine trends. Kept minimal.

Early nadir RBC count captures acute anemia/hemodilution and bleeding/transfusion exposure, which are associated with postoperative AKI. Conservative due to 0–12h transient effects and collinearity with mean.

A low RBC nadir during 13–24h suggests ongoing anemia/bleeding and potential transfusion/inflammation, which can impair renal oxygen delivery and increase AKI risk. Minimum is preferred over mean/max to capture the insult.

Low RBC count late (25–36h) can reflect ongoing bleeding/hemodilution and reduced oxygen delivery, which may worsen renal medullary hypoxia and AKI risk. Use the minimum as the most relevant insult marker versus mean/max.

Measurement frequency mainly reflects routine post-op monitoring and clinician concern rather than a direct AKI mechanism. Early (0–12h) ordering is common after cardiac surgery and is weakly specific for later creatinine rise.
More frequent checks may indicate instability/bleeding and higher acuity, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Still primarily a behavioral proxy and kept <0.05.

Persistent need for repeated RBC monitoring late suggests ongoing bleeding/hemodynamic issues that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. As a proxy variable (not physiology), it remains capped below 0.05.
Ordering D-dimer early is nonspecific and often protocolized; it does not directly reflect renal injury. As a measured/proxy feature in the immediate post-op window, it has minimal predictive value.
D-dimer ordering may reflect concern for thrombosis/PE or inflammatory coagulopathy, which can correlate with severity of illness and AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy and kept <0.05.

Late D-dimer ordering suggests ongoing concern for thrombotic complications or systemic inflammation, indirectly linked to renal hypoperfusion/microthrombi risk. As a proxy variable, importance remains low (<0.05).
TEG ACT measurement early is common after CPB to guide heparin reversal/bleeding management and is not specific for AKI. As a measured feature in 0–12h, it is weakly informative.
Continued TEG ACT testing may indicate ongoing bleeding/coagulopathy and higher illness severity, which can be associated with AKI. However it remains an ordering proxy and is capped <0.05.

Late need for TEG ACT suggests persistent coagulopathy/bleeding risk and potential hemodynamic compromise that can contribute to AKI. Still primarily reflects clinician behavior rather than a direct renal biomarker, so kept <0.05.

Immature granulocytes reflect systemic inflammation/stress, which can correlate with AKI risk, but early post-CPB inflammation is common and often transient. Mean is less informative than extremes for acute inflammatory surges, so kept low.

Sustained inflammatory response in the 13–24h trajectory phase can signal evolving infection/SIRS, which increases AKI risk via cytokine-mediated injury and hemodynamic effects. Mean still smooths peaks, so importance remains modest.

A high early peak may indicate a strong inflammatory response, which is plausibly linked to AKI, but 0–12h post-CPB peaks are frequent and nonspecific. Max is preferred over mean/min for capturing the inflammatory insult, but capped conservatively in this epoch.

A high peak in immature granulocytes during 13–24h suggests worsening inflammation/infection rather than transient CPB effect, which is more predictive of subsequent AKI. Max best captures the pathologic surge versus mean/min, so it is weighted higher within the epoch cap.
The minimum immature granulocyte percentage is not a plausible trigger or marker for AKI and is largely uninformative. Also redundant given max/mean options.
Low/min immature granulocytes do not indicate AKI risk and add little beyond other aggregations. Kept at minimal importance to avoid collinearity with max/mean.
Measurement frequency of immature granulocytes mainly reflects clinician concern for infection/inflammation rather than renal physiology. Early post-op ordering is common and nonspecific for AKI.
Ordering frequency is a weak behavioral proxy for acuity and not a direct driver of creatinine rise. At 13–24h it remains nonspecific for renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity.
More frequent checks may indicate evolving sepsis/SIRS which can contribute to AKI, but this is indirect and highly confounded. Keep low to avoid over-weighting clinician behavior.

TEG fibrinolysis testing reflects bleeding/coagulopathy management and is not a direct predictor of renal injury. In the immediate 0–12h window it is especially nonspecific for later creatinine ratio.
Continued TEG assessment suggests ongoing hemostatic issues/transfusion needs, which only indirectly relate to AKI risk. Not a primary renal perfusion marker.
Late TEG use may signal persistent bleeding/transfusion/inflammation that can worsen renal outcomes, but the link is indirect. Maintain low importance for sparsity.

Early average creatinine ratio can reflect immediate post-op renal stress, but values in 0–12h are often transient and diluted by fluid shifts. Cap to reduce redundancy versus more informative later/extreme summaries.

A sustained elevation in creatinine ratio during 13–24h suggests early AKI trajectory rather than transient perioperative effects. Mean is still less sensitive than extremes/late window but has established prognostic value.

Creatinine ratio in the 25–36h leading-indicator window is a direct early biomarker of evolving AKI and strongly predicts the 60h creatinine ratio. The mean captures persistent renal dysfunction close to the outcome.

An early peak creatinine ratio may indicate acute renal insult, but immediate post-op fluctuations and dilutional effects are common; conservative scoring per 0–12h cap. Max better captures insult than mean/min in this epoch.

A peak creatinine ratio in 13–24h reflects worsening renal function beyond immediate perioperative noise and is a strong early AKI signal. Max is preferred over mean/min to capture clinically meaningful deterioration.

A high peak creatinine ratio at 25–36h is a definitive direct biomarker of ongoing AKI and is highly predictive of the 60h ratio. This extreme summary best captures clinically relevant renal deterioration closest to the outcome.
The minimum creatinine ratio early post-op is typically uninformative (often reflects hemodilution) and does not indicate renal injury. De-emphasized to avoid collinearity with max/mean.
A low minimum creatinine ratio is not a marker of AKI and is often driven by fluid balance rather than renal recovery. Keep minimal to reduce redundancy with max/mean.

Even in the late window, the minimum is less informative than the peak or sustained elevation for predicting AKI progression. Small nonzero weight acknowledges possible recovery signal but remains low for sparsity/collinearity control.
More frequent creatinine checks may reflect clinician concern for renal dysfunction, but it is a behavioral proxy and confounded by overall acuity. Kept <0.05 per measurement-frequency rule.

Measurement frequency reflects clinician concern and evolving acuity rather than renal physiology itself. In the 13–24h window it may weakly proxy early renal dysfunction workup, but should remain low to avoid behavioral confounding.

More frequent creatinine checks closer to hour 60 can indicate suspected AKI or hemodynamic instability, but it is still a care-process variable rather than a biomarker. Kept <0.05 per rule for _measured features.

Hyperoncotic albumin use early post-op may reflect hypotension/vasoplegia or capillary leak, indirectly linked to AKI risk. In the immediate 0–12h period this is common and nonspecific, so conservative scoring.

Ongoing need for 25% albumin in 13–24h can signal persistent third-spacing or hemodynamic support needs, which can correlate with renal hypoperfusion. Still an indirect marker and treatment choice, not a direct AKI predictor.

Late requirement for 25% albumin (25–36h) suggests continued instability/volume management challenges closer to the outcome window, which can associate with worsening renal perfusion. Remains below strong-predictor range because it is not specific and may be confounded by practice patterns.
5% albumin is often used for routine post-bypass volume resuscitation and is not nephrotoxic. Early use is common and nonspecific for subsequent AKI, so low importance.
Continued colloid resuscitation in 13–24h may indicate ongoing vasoplegia/third-spacing and potential renal hypoperfusion. It is an indirect severity marker and highly practice-dependent.

Albumin use in 25–36h can reflect persistent fluid management issues closer to hour 60, modestly associated with AKI risk via hemodynamic instability. Still nonspecific and treatment-driven, so kept low.

Acetaminophen/codeine is not a typical driver of postoperative AKI; any association would be indirect (pain control/sedation effects). Early dosing is routine and provides little renal prognostic information.
Not a recognized AKI risk factor in this context; opioid component may affect hemodynamics minimally but is not a primary mechanism. Likely reflects analgesia practice rather than renal injury.
Late use remains primarily an analgesia marker without direct renal relevance. Any link to creatinine rise would be weak and confounded.
Acetaminophen is generally kidney-safe at therapeutic doses and is routine post-op. Provides minimal signal for AKI risk, especially in the immediate post-surgery window.
Continued acetaminophen use is common and not mechanistically tied to renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity in typical dosing. Low predictive value for creatinine_ratio.
Analgesic dosing late post-op is not a meaningful predictor of AKI compared with hemodynamics, fluids, and renal labs. Kept very low to maintain sparsity.

Allopurinol use mainly reflects chronic gout/hyperuricemia management and is not an acute postoperative AKI driver. Early continuation/holding is practice-dependent with weak linkage to creatinine rise.
Ongoing allopurinol administration is a comorbidity/medication-history marker rather than a perioperative renal insult. Any association with AKI is indirect and likely minimal.

Allopurinol use in this window is usually chronic gout management and is not a typical driver of post-cardiac surgery AKI. Dose given is more reflective of home-med continuation than renal perfusion or tubular injury.

Aminocaproic acid is used for bleeding prophylaxis/management and has no strong direct link to AKI compared with hemodynamics and nephrotoxins. In the immediate 0–12h post-op period, its use is common and not specific for renal injury.

Ongoing antifibrinolytic use may weakly proxy bleeding/re-exploration risk and illness severity, which can secondarily affect renal perfusion. It is still not a primary or established predictor of AKI.

Late continued use may indicate persistent bleeding/instability and higher transfusion/vasopressor needs, indirectly increasing AKI risk. The medication itself is not a direct nephrotoxin, so importance remains low.

Amiodarone use early post-op typically reflects atrial/ventricular arrhythmias; arrhythmias can reduce cardiac output and renal perfusion. In 0–12h, arrhythmias are common/transient, so predictive value is limited.

Need for amiodarone in 13–24h suggests persistent arrhythmia burden and potential low-output physiology, which can contribute to AKI. This is an indirect marker and should remain capped to avoid over-weighting.

Continued amiodarone requirement at 25–36h can indicate ongoing hemodynamic compromise (e.g., AF with RVR, ventricular ectopy) and reduced renal perfusion closer to the outcome window. It is not a direct biomarker, but a meaningful established risk-context signal.

Amlodipine is a chronic antihypertensive and is often held or variably resumed post-op; it is not a typical acute determinant of AKI. Early administration is unlikely to reflect renal injury mechanisms.
Resumption of amlodipine mainly reflects medication reconciliation and blood pressure management rather than renal perfusion failure. It has minimal specific prognostic value for AKI.

Late amlodipine use may weakly indicate hemodynamic stability (tolerating oral meds), which could correlate with lower AKI risk, but this is nonspecific. Keep low to avoid confounding by care patterns.
Ampicillin is not a common nephrotoxin in typical dosing and early post-op antibiotics are often prophylactic. Its presence is more standard-of-care than a renal risk signal.

Continuation may indicate suspected infection, which can contribute to AKI via inflammation and hemodynamic effects, but this is indirect. Ampicillin itself is not strongly nephrotoxic compared with agents like vancomycin/aminoglycosides.

Antibiotic use closer to 60h may reflect evolving infection/sepsis risk, which can precipitate AKI through hypotension and cytokine-mediated injury. Still an indirect proxy and not a definitive renal biomarker.

Angiotensin II use indicates vasodilatory shock requiring advanced vasopressor support, a strong context for renal hypoperfusion and AKI. In 0–12h, shock may be transient post-CPB, so cap per epoch rule.

Requirement for angiotensin II at 13–24h suggests persistent vasoplegia/shock and high vasopressor burden, which is strongly associated with subsequent AKI. This is a high-value hemodynamic severity marker in the trajectory phase.

Angiotensin II use at 25–36h is a leading indicator of ongoing refractory shock and severe renal hypoperfusion close to the 60h creatinine measurement. This level of circulatory failure is a direct pathway to ischemic tubular injury and strongly predicts creatinine rise.

Apixaban dosing is not a direct driver of postoperative AKI; it mainly reflects anticoagulation strategy/bleeding risk. In the immediate 0–12h window it is unlikely to add specific prognostic signal for creatinine rise.

Ongoing apixaban use may weakly proxy clinical stability and ability to take/receive anticoagulation, but has no strong mechanistic link to AKI. Keep low to avoid over-weighting treatment-pattern confounding.

Later apixaban dosing can indicate recovery trajectory (hemostasis, enteral tolerance) and overall stability, indirectly related to AKI risk. Still not nephrotoxic and not a direct renal biomarker, so remain low.
Aspirin at typical post-cardiac surgery doses is not a primary cause of AKI and is often routine. Early administration mainly reflects protocolized care rather than renal perfusion status.
Continuation of aspirin may weakly reflect clinical stability and absence of major bleeding, but does not directly predict creatinine rise. Indirect association only, so low inclusion probability.
Later aspirin use remains largely protocol-driven and not a direct AKI mechanism. Any signal is likely confounded by overall recovery, so keep low.
Statin dosing is not an acute determinant of postoperative renal perfusion and is commonly continued as standard care. Rhabdomyolysis-related AKI is rare and not captured well by routine dosing.
Atorvastatin continuation is a weak proxy for stability and medication reconciliation rather than kidney injury physiology. Minimal expected incremental value for predicting creatinine_ratio.
Later statin dosing remains non-specific for AKI risk and is unlikely to precede creatinine rise mechanistically. Keep very low for sparsity.
Bisacodyl use reflects bowel regimen practices and does not meaningfully relate to renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity. Any association would be incidental.
Bowel regimen administration is not a physiologic marker for AKI and is heavily practice-pattern driven. Keep near-zero importance.
Bisacodyl remains unrelated to AKI mechanisms; at most it indicates enteral tolerance. Not expected to add predictive value for creatinine_ratio.
Bivalirudin use can indicate HIT concern or complex anticoagulation needs, which may correlate with critical illness and renal hypoperfusion risk. In 0–12h, cap remains conservative and indirect.

Ongoing bivalirudin suggests persistent high-acuity anticoagulation needs and may co-occur with shock/ECMO or thrombosis concerns that increase AKI risk. Still largely a treatment proxy rather than direct renal marker.
In the 25–36h leading window, continued bivalirudin may better reflect ongoing severe illness and organ support contexts associated with AKI. It is not nephrotoxic itself, so keep modest.

Early loop diuretic requirement can reflect volume overload, venous congestion, and low effective renal perfusion after cardiac surgery, all linked to AKI risk. In 0–12h this is common and partly reactive, so keep below the early-window cap.

Loop diuretic requirement in the trajectory phase can reflect evolving volume overload/venous congestion or oliguria, both associated with AKI risk after cardiac surgery. It is also partly treatment-driven, so not definitive.

Nearer to the outcome window, escalating bumetanide use often signals persistent congestion or diuretic-resistant oliguria, which correlates with worsening renal function. Still confounded by clinician response and fluid strategy.

Calcium acetate is a phosphate binder and is not a typical early post-op driver or marker of AKI in the immediate 0–12h period. Use is uncommon and more reflective of chronic kidney disease management.
May indicate underlying CKD/mineral bone disease rather than acute renal perfusion injury. Limited direct linkage to short-term creatinine rise.
Could reflect known CKD or concern for hyperphosphatemia, indirectly associated with AKI risk, but it is not a direct physiologic predictor of creatinine rise at 60h.
Typically used for supplementation/antacid or chronic phosphate binding and does not directly predict AKI in the immediate post-op window. More related to baseline comorbidity and routine care.
Weak indirect association via underlying CKD or electrolyte management; not a direct marker of renal injury. Largely treatment-pattern driven.
May correlate with CKD/mineral metabolism issues, which increase AKI susceptibility, but calcium carbonate dosing itself is not a strong predictor of acute creatinine change.

Calcium chloride use early post-op often reflects hemodynamic instability, massive transfusion, or post-CPB electrolyte derangements; these contexts can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. However, 0–12h derangements are often transient, so keep conservative.

Ongoing need for calcium chloride beyond the immediate post-op period can indicate persistent instability or significant electrolyte abnormalities, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Still a nonspecific treatment marker.

Late calcium chloride requirement may signal continued critical illness (vasoplegia, transfusion needs, severe hypocalcemia) that tracks with renal injury risk. Not a direct biomarker, but closer temporal proximity increases relevance.

Often given for hypocalcemia or hyperkalemia stabilization; these can accompany shock or early renal dysfunction, but are common and transient immediately after surgery. Thus only modest predictive value in 0–12h.
Continued calcium gluconate use may reflect persistent electrolyte disturbances or hyperkalemia management, which can co-occur with evolving AKI. Still indirect and treatment-driven.

Later calcium gluconate administration can be a proxy for hyperkalemia or ongoing critical illness, both associated with worsening renal function approaching hour 60. Not specific enough for high inclusion.
Beta-blocker administration early post-op is mainly chronic medication continuation and is not a direct driver of AKI. Immediate post-op dosing is often held/variable and weakly informative.
Continuation may reflect hemodynamic stability rather than renal injury risk; any association with AKI is indirect via cardiac output/blood pressure. Overall weak predictor for creatinine ratio.
Beta-blockade can contribute to bradycardia/low cardiac output and reduced renal perfusion, but carvedilol dosing in ICU is usually not a primary AKI driver compared with shock/vasopressors.
Cefepime is not a classic nephrotoxin; early post-op antibiotic choice/dose is more reflective of infection risk than renal injury, and 0–12h is a transient perioperative period.
Ongoing broad-spectrum antibiotics may proxy evolving infection/sepsis (a contributor to AKI), but cefepime itself is not strongly nephrotoxic and is often dose-adjusted for renal function.

In the leading-indicator window, continued cefepime can reflect suspected infection/sepsis and higher acuity, which can worsen renal perfusion; still an indirect marker rather than a direct AKI biomarker.
Ceftriaxone has minimal direct nephrotoxicity and early perioperative administration is common; weak mechanistic link to subsequent creatinine rise.
May indicate treatment of infection, which can contribute to AKI, but ceftriaxone itself is not a strong renal insult and is an indirect proxy at best.
Continued antibiotic use closer to outcome may reflect ongoing infection/inflammation and higher illness severity, indirectly increasing AKI risk; not a direct nephrotoxin.
Clonidine can lower sympathetic tone and blood pressure, potentially reducing renal perfusion, but early post-op dosing is usually limited and hemodynamic effects are managed with pressors/fluids.
Persistent clonidine use may contribute to hypotension/bradycardia and lower renal perfusion, but it is not a primary established AKI predictor compared with MAP/vasopressor needs.
In the 25–36h window, clonidine-associated hypotension/bradycardia could more meaningfully track low-perfusion states preceding AKI, though still an indirect and modifiable contributor.
Antiplatelet therapy has no direct physiologic link to AKI; early administration is more related to cardiac indications than renal injury.
Clopidogrel use is largely unrelated to renal perfusion or tubular injury and is unlikely to add predictive value for creatinine rise.
Continued clopidogrel remains weakly related to AKI risk; any association would be confounding by indication rather than causal renal injury.
Colchicine is not a typical perioperative AKI driver; early dosing is uncommon and any renal relevance is mainly via dose adjustment in existing renal dysfunction.
May reflect treatment of pericarditis/inflammation, but colchicine itself is not a strong nephrotoxin; renal association is indirect and generally weak.
In the leading window, colchicine use could weakly proxy ongoing inflammatory complications, but it is not a direct biomarker or established driver of postoperative AKI.
D10 in LR is primarily a maintenance/anti-hypoglycemia fluid choice and is not a direct AKI driver. In the immediate 0–12h post-op window, its use is common and weakly specific for renal injury.
Ongoing need for dextrose-containing crystalloid may reflect nutrition/insulin management rather than renal hypoperfusion. At most it is an indirect marker of illness severity and fluid strategy.

Later use could correlate with continued ICU support and fluid administration, indirectly relating to AKI risk via volume status/hemodilution. Still not a nephrotoxic exposure or direct renal biomarker.
D10W use early post-op is typically for glucose management and does not directly affect renal tubular injury. In 0–12h it is common and nonspecific for subsequent creatinine rise.

Higher D10W volume may reflect ongoing metabolic management and overall fluid exposure, which can confound creatinine via dilution but is not a primary AKI mechanism. It is an indirect severity/management signal.
Continued D10W later may indicate persistent critical illness or feeding/insulin issues, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Not a direct hemodynamic or nephrotoxic predictor.

Hypotonic dextrose-saline volume reflects fluid strategy and can influence intravascular volume/osmolality but is not a direct AKI trigger. In the immediate post-op period, fluid choice is often protocolized and nonspecific.

Use in 13–24h is more reflective of maintenance fluid selection than renal perfusion failure. Any association with creatinine_ratio is likely indirect through overall fluid balance and illness severity.
Later hypotonic maintenance fluids may correlate with ongoing ICU needs and fluid administration, indirectly affecting creatinine via dilution/volume status. Still weakly specific for intrinsic AKI.
D5LR indicates crystalloid administration and maintenance glucose support, which is not a direct renal injury mechanism. Early post-op crystalloid use is common and poorly discriminative for AKI.
Continued D5LR suggests ongoing fluid requirements but does not directly capture hypotension, low cardiac output, or nephrotoxin exposure. Thus it is only a weak indirect predictor.

Greater crystalloid volume closer to the outcome can reflect persistent hemodynamic instability or attempts to treat low perfusion, indirectly linked to AKI risk. It may also affect measured creatinine via hemodilution, but remains nonspecific.
D5NS volume is a general maintenance/resuscitation fluid and not a direct biomarker of renal injury. In 0–12h, fluid administration is routine and weakly predictive of later creatinine rise.
Ongoing D5NS use is mainly a management choice and only indirectly related to AKI through volume status and severity of illness. It lacks specificity for renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity.

Later continued crystalloid needs may indicate ongoing shock/third spacing or attempts to optimize renal perfusion, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Still not a direct renal biomarker, so kept low for sparsity.
D50W is typically given for hypoglycemia treatment and has no direct causal link to AKI. In the immediate post-op window it is an episodic intervention and nonspecific for renal outcomes.
D50W use mainly reflects hypoglycemia treatment or insulin protocols rather than renal perfusion or tubular injury. At most it is an indirect marker of illness severity and should remain sparse.

Later need for D50W can indicate ongoing metabolic instability (e.g., insulin infusion, hepatic dysfunction) and higher acuity, which may correlate with AKI risk. Still not a direct renal mechanism, so keep low.
D5W volume is typically maintenance/medication carrier and does not directly predict AKI. Early postoperative fluids are common and nonspecific in the 0–12h window.
D5W administration is generally routine and weakly linked to kidney injury except through overall fluid management. Not a specific driver of creatinine rise.
In the later window, higher D5W may reflect attempts to correct hypernatremia or manage free-water balance, indirectly tracking severity. Still not a direct AKI predictor.

Dialysate infusion implies renal replacement therapy initiation or preparation, which is strongly associated with significant renal dysfunction. Capped at 0.10 due to the conservative 0–12h epoch limit.
Ongoing dialysate use in 13–24h indicates established severe AKI/renal failure requiring RRT, closely linked to higher creatinine at 60h. This is a near-direct marker of the outcome trajectory.

Dialysate infusion at 25–36h is essentially a definitive indicator of severe AKI requiring RRT and strongly predicts elevated creatinine ratio by 60h. It reflects renal failure rather than a nonspecific treatment.
Opioid PCA dosing is primarily analgesia-related and not a direct AKI driver. Any association would be confounding by severity or ventilation status and should be minimal.
Hydromorphone PCA use reflects pain control practices and patient wakefulness rather than renal perfusion. Weak and indirect relationship to AKI risk.
Later PCA dosing remains a care-pattern variable with little mechanistic link to creatinine rise. Keep very low to preserve sparsity.
Systemic hydromorphone dose is not nephrotoxic and does not directly affect renal perfusion at typical doses. Early postoperative opioid needs are common and nonspecific.
Ongoing opioid dosing mainly reflects pain/sedation strategy and may correlate with ventilation but not kidney injury. Indirect/confounded signal only.
Hydromorphone dosing in this window is still not a recognized AKI predictor and is heavily confounded by care practices. Maintain minimal inclusion probability.

Dobutamine use indicates low cardiac output/cardiogenic shock physiology, which can reduce renal perfusion and precipitate AKI. Limited by the 0–12h epoch cap and early transient postoperative instability.

Persistent inotrope requirement at 13–24h suggests ongoing low-output state and renal hypoperfusion, an established AKI risk factor after cardiac surgery. Moderately predictive but not a direct biomarker.

Ongoing dobutamine requirement at 25–36h suggests persistent low cardiac output/cardiogenic shock physiology, which can reduce renal perfusion and precipitate AKI. Dose intensity can proxy severity of hemodynamic compromise close to the 60h creatinine outcome.
Docusate is not nephrotoxic and does not meaningfully affect renal perfusion; use mainly reflects routine bowel regimen. Early post-op administration has no established link to AKI.
Docusate use is routine supportive care and not mechanistically related to kidney injury. At most it weakly reflects general ICU care patterns rather than AKI risk.
Docusate is clinically unrelated to renal injury and is not a marker of hemodynamic instability or nephrotoxin exposure. Any association would be incidental.

Dopamine use early after surgery can indicate hypotension/low cardiac output requiring vasoactive support, which is linked to renal hypoperfusion. However, 0–12h is often transient post-bypass instability, so predictive value is limited.
Continued dopamine requirement at 13–24h suggests ongoing shock/poor perfusion beyond immediate post-op effects, increasing AKI risk. Dose may capture severity of hemodynamic support needs.

Dopamine requirement at 25–36h is a strong indicator of persistent hemodynamic compromise close to the outcome window, which directly threatens renal perfusion. Higher doses typically reflect more severe shock physiology and higher AKI risk.

Enoxaparin is not a typical cause of AKI and early post-op dosing is driven by VTE prophylaxis protocols rather than renal physiology. Any relationship is likely confounded by clinical practice patterns.
Enoxaparin dosing may indirectly reflect renal function (dose adjustments/withholding in CKD), but it is not a direct AKI driver. Association is likely weak and practice-dependent.

Later enoxaparin use can weakly proxy clinician comfort with renal function and bleeding risk, but it is not mechanistically predictive of creatinine rise. Keep low to avoid over-weighting a practice-pattern variable.

Ephedrine use suggests transient hypotension treated with bolus vasopressor, which can relate to renal perfusion but is common immediately post-op. In the 0–12h window it is often short-lived and less predictive of sustained AKI.

Ephedrine use beyond the immediate post-op period may indicate intermittent hypotension, which can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Still, it is a nonspecific and typically low-intensity marker compared with continuous vasoactive infusions.

Ephedrine use at 25–36h may reflect ongoing hemodynamic lability closer to the creatinine outcome, plausibly increasing AKI risk. As a bolus agent it remains a relatively indirect marker compared with sustained vasopressor/inotrope needs.
Esmolol is typically used for tachyarrhythmias or rate control and is not a direct AKI driver. In the immediate post-op period its use is common and not specific for renal risk.

Esmolol may indirectly reflect atrial fibrillation or sympathetic stress, which can correlate with illness severity, but it does not directly predict renal tubular injury. Any association with AKI is likely weak and confounded.

Later esmolol use can indicate persistent arrhythmia management and ongoing physiologic stress, which may correlate with worse outcomes including AKI. It remains an indirect marker and should be kept low for sparsity.

Etomidate is not nephrotoxic; any association with AKI would be indirect via peri-intubation hemodynamics and illness severity. In the immediate 0–12h post-op window, this is common and transient, so predictive value is limited.
Use in this epoch may reflect re-intubation/procedural sedation and higher acuity, which can correlate with AKI risk indirectly. Still not a direct renal mechanism.

Late need for etomidate suggests ongoing instability or procedures closer to the outcome window, indirectly increasing AKI risk through hemodynamic stress. Not a direct biomarker, so kept low for sparsity.
Ezetimibe has no meaningful acute effect on renal perfusion or tubular injury in the ICU setting. Likely reflects chronic lipid management rather than AKI physiology.
Continuation of ezetimibe is routine medication reconciliation and not mechanistically linked to postoperative AKI. Minimal prognostic value for creatinine rise.
Ezetimibe dosing remains unrelated to acute kidney injury mechanisms and is unlikely to predict creatinine_ratio. Kept near-zero to maintain sparsity.
Famotidine is standard stress-ulcer prophylaxis and not a driver of AKI; dose may be adjusted in renal dysfunction but typically not this early. Weak indirect signal at most.
Ongoing famotidine use is routine; any association would be via renal-dose adjustment practices rather than causation. Indirect and modest.
By 25–36h, dose reduction/holding could reflect emerging renal dysfunction and clinician response, giving a slightly stronger indirect signal. Still primarily a care-pattern proxy, so kept <0.05.
Opioid PCA dosing mainly reflects analgesia needs and sedation strategy, not renal injury; fentanyl is generally hemodynamically stable. Early post-op dosing is common and weakly informative.
Higher PCA requirements may correlate with complications (e.g., agitation, respiratory issues) and overall severity, indirectly relating to AKI risk. Not a direct renal predictor.
Persistent or escalating opioid needs late may indicate ongoing critical illness and prolonged ventilation/immobility, indirectly associated with AKI. Still non-specific and kept low.
Fentanyl dosing in the immediate post-op period is routine for sedation/analgesia and not nephrotoxic. Any AKI link would be indirect via hypotension/illness severity, so minimal.
Continued higher fentanyl may reflect ongoing ventilation and hemodynamic fragility, which can correlate with AKI risk indirectly. Non-specific signal.
Late sedation/analgesia requirements can be a proxy for persistent critical illness closer to the 60h creatinine outcome. Not mechanistically renal, so capped low for sparsity.
Intranasal fluticasone has negligible systemic effect and no plausible acute link to renal perfusion or tubular injury. Represents outpatient medication continuation without AKI relevance.
Intranasal corticosteroid dosing has no meaningful physiologic link to renal perfusion or tubular injury in the ICU. Use mainly reflects outpatient/allergy management rather than AKI risk.
Intranasal fluticasone is not nephrotoxic and does not materially affect hemodynamics or kidney function. Likely unrelated to creatinine rise at 60h.

Folic acid administration is not a driver of postoperative AKI and is typically routine supplementation. In the immediate 0–12h window it provides no specific prognostic signal for creatinine trajectory.
Folic acid is not associated with acute changes in renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity. Ordering is unlikely to reflect AKI concern.
Folic acid dosing does not directly influence postoperative creatinine rise. It is generally unrelated to AKI mechanisms in this timeframe.

Loop diuretic use early after cardiac surgery often reflects volume overload/low cardiac output management and can contribute to intravascular depletion, both relevant to AKI risk. However, in 0–12h effects can be transient and confounded by routine diuresis, so capped conservatively.

Ongoing furosemide requirement in 13–24h may indicate persistent congestion or worsening hemodynamics and can worsen renal perfusion if over-diuresed. This trajectory-phase signal is more informative for later creatinine rise than the immediate postoperative period.

High diuretic requirement close to the outcome window can be a strong marker of evolving cardiorenal syndrome or attempts to manage oliguria/volume overload. It is also a potential contributor to prerenal azotemia via intravascular depletion, making it a leading indicator for creatinine_ratio.

Gabapentin is not a typical cause of AKI and is usually given for analgesia; dose is more related to pain regimen than renal injury. Any association is indirect (dose adjustment in CKD) and weak in the immediate postoperative window.
Gabapentin dosing may weakly reflect baseline renal function via dose reduction, but it is not a driver of postoperative AKI. Overall signal for creatinine_ratio is indirect and small.
Later gabapentin dosing could reflect clinician adjustment for reduced GFR, but remains an indirect proxy rather than a causal AKI factor. Expected incremental predictive value is low.
Guaifenesin is not nephrotoxic and does not affect renal perfusion. Use reflects secretion management rather than AKI risk.
Guaifenesin dosing has no established relationship to postoperative AKI mechanisms. It is unlikely to add predictive value for creatinine_ratio.
Expect no physiologic linkage between guaifenesin and creatinine rise after cardiac surgery. Any association would be incidental.

Early hypotonic fluid administration relates to resuscitation/maintenance strategy and can correlate with overall fluid exposure, which may affect renal congestion and perfusion indirectly. In 0–12h this is highly confounded by routine postoperative fluid management, so kept low.

Continued half-normal saline use may reflect ongoing fluid needs and net positive balance, indirectly linked to venous congestion and AKI risk. Still a nonspecific marker compared with direct hemodynamic/renal labs, so capped below 0.05.

IV fluid choice/amount can influence volume status and renal perfusion, but half-normal saline is not specifically nephrotoxic and is often routine maintenance. Signal is indirect and confounded by overall resuscitation strategy.

Heparin dosing early post-op is usually protocol-driven (CPB/line patency) and not a direct AKI mechanism. Any association would be indirect via bleeding/transfusion or HIT, which is uncommon this early.
Ongoing heparin may reflect thrombosis prophylaxis or anticoagulation needs; renal impact is indirect. HIT-related AKI is possible but relatively rare and typically not captured well by dose alone.

Later heparin use may indicate higher acuity (e.g., mechanical support/AF anticoagulation), but it is not a direct renal biomarker. Dose is a weak proxy for complications that could affect kidney perfusion.

Hydralazine can lower afterload and blood pressure, potentially reducing renal perfusion if hypotension occurs, but early post-op use is variable and often transient. In the 0–12h epoch, derangements are common and less predictive.

Need for antihypertensive therapy may reflect hemodynamic instability or aggressive BP control that could affect renal perfusion. Still an indirect marker and confounded by clinician targets and concomitant agents.

In the 25–36h window, antihypertensive requirement may better reflect ongoing hemodynamic management that could influence renal perfusion. However, hydralazine dose alone is not a strong or specific AKI predictor.
Opioid/acetaminophen analgesia is routine and not a direct driver of AKI at typical doses. Any renal association would be indirect (sedation-related hypotension), which is weak and nonspecific.
Analgesic dosing mainly reflects pain control practices rather than renal physiology. Acetaminophen is generally kidney-safe compared with NSAIDs, so predictive value for AKI is minimal.
Continued use remains largely routine and not mechanistically linked to postoperative creatinine rise. Dose is more a marker of pain management than renal injury.

Hydrocortisone early post-op may indicate vasoplegia/shock treatment, which is associated with renal hypoperfusion, but the 0–12h period is noisy and transient. Steroid itself is not a direct AKI biomarker.

Ongoing hydrocortisone suggests persistent vasoplegia or refractory shock requiring support, a known AKI risk context. Still indirect because it reflects severity of illness rather than renal injury per se.

Hydrocortisone use in the 25–36h window can be a stronger marker of ongoing shock/vasopressor dependence, which directly threatens renal perfusion and increases AKI risk. It remains a proxy for hemodynamic collapse rather than a kidney biomarker.
Hypertonic saline use is uncommon and may reflect severe hyponatremia or intracranial concerns; renal relevance is indirect. Early post-op administration is not a specific predictor of AKI.
May indicate significant electrolyte derangement or aggressive osmotherapy, which can accompany high acuity and fluid shifts affecting kidneys. Still nonspecific and heavily confounded by indication.

Later hypertonic saline suggests ongoing severe derangements and complex fluid/electrolyte management that can correlate with AKI risk. However, it is not a direct nephrotoxin and remains an indirect severity marker.

NSAIDs can reduce renal prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriolar dilation and worsen renal perfusion, increasing AKI risk. Early post-op exposure is relevant but the 0–12h window is capped and often limited in ICU practice.

Ongoing NSAID exposure during the trajectory phase can contribute to AKI, especially with hypovolemia or low cardiac output. Still an indirect-to-established risk factor rather than a direct biomarker.

NSAID dosing closest to the outcome window may meaningfully worsen creatinine rise by impairing renal autoregulation. This is an established medication-related AKI risk factor in vulnerable post-cardiac surgery patients.
Inhaled acetylcysteine is used for airway secretion management and has no direct renal perfusion or nephrotoxic effect. Any association would be confounding by pulmonary disease severity.
Primarily reflects respiratory secretion burden and care patterns rather than kidney injury mechanisms. Not an established predictor of postoperative creatinine rise.
Continued mucolytic use is not mechanistically linked to AKI and is unlikely to add predictive value beyond overall illness severity. Keep very low for sparsity.
Inhaled LABA use indicates obstructive lung disease/bronchospasm management and does not directly affect renal function. Any relationship to AKI would be indirect via overall acuity.
Represents pulmonary therapy rather than renal physiology; not a known driver of postoperative AKI. Potential confounding by COPD severity only.
No direct nephrotoxic or renal hemodynamic effect expected from inhaled arformoterol at typical doses. Maintain minimal inclusion probability.
Inhaled corticosteroids have minimal systemic absorption and are not typical contributors to AKI. Use mainly reflects chronic airway disease management.
Not mechanistically linked to renal hypoperfusion or tubular injury; association would be indirect at best. Keep near-zero for sparsity.
Continued inhaled steroid therapy is unlikely to predict creatinine rise independent of other severity markers. No established AKI signal.
Short-acting bronchodilator use reflects bronchospasm/airway management and does not directly cause AKI. Any effect would be indirect via respiratory failure severity.
Not a renal biomarker or nephrotoxin; primarily a marker of pulmonary status and treatment intensity. Low expected incremental value for AKI prediction.
Ongoing bronchodilator requirement is not a direct driver of creatinine increase. Keep very low to avoid adding non-specific acuity proxies.
Nebulized saline is supportive airway care without direct renal implications. Any correlation with AKI would be weak and confounded by respiratory illness.
Inhaled saline is primarily for airway secretion management and has no direct renal perfusion or nephrotoxic effect. Dose mainly reflects respiratory care intensity rather than AKI biology.

Inhaled saline use is not a known driver of postoperative AKI and does not meaningfully affect intravascular volume or renal hemodynamics. More a marker of pulmonary toileting needs than kidney injury risk.

Inhaled beta-agonists have minimal direct impact on renal function; any effects (e.g., mild tachycardia, potassium shifts) are indirect. In the immediate 0–12h post-op window, this is unlikely to add specific AKI signal.

Levalbuterol dose may weakly proxy respiratory failure severity and catecholamine physiology, which can correlate with overall illness. However it is not an established AKI predictor and remains indirect.

Ongoing bronchodilator requirement can reflect persistent pulmonary dysfunction and higher overall acuity, which may co-occur with renal hypoperfusion. Still an indirect marker rather than a causal AKI driver.
Basal insulin use reflects diabetes/stress hyperglycemia management, a background AKI risk context but not a direct perioperative renal insult. Early post-op dosing is often protocol-driven.

Continued basal insulin suggests ongoing hyperglycemia/diabetes burden, which is an established AKI risk factor but not strongly predictive over short horizons. Dose is influenced by glycemic protocols and nutrition.
Later basal insulin requirement may indicate persistent metabolic stress and diabetes severity, modestly associated with AKI risk. Still indirect and not a renal biomarker.
Detemir use similarly reflects diabetes/stress hyperglycemia rather than renal injury mechanisms. In 0–12h, dosing is commonly routine and not specific for AKI prediction.
Basal insulin requirement in this epoch is a weak proxy for diabetes and ongoing stress response, which can increase AKI susceptibility. Not a direct hemodynamic or nephrotoxic exposure.
Persistent basal insulin needs may track sustained illness severity and diabetes burden, modestly linked to AKI. Effect remains indirect and confounded by feeding and insulin protocols.

Short-acting insulin dose reflects stress hyperglycemia, which correlates with postoperative complications including AKI but is not specific. In the immediate post-op period, glucose variability is common and transient.

Higher prandial/correction insulin needs can indicate persistent hyperglycemia and systemic stress/inflammation, which are associated with AKI risk. Still an indirect predictor and influenced by nutrition and protocols.

Ongoing elevated correction insulin requirements closer to the outcome may better reflect sustained stress response and severity of illness, modestly increasing AKI risk. Remains nonspecific and protocol-dependent.
Need for insulin infusion suggests significant stress hyperglycemia and higher acuity, which can correlate with AKI risk. In 0–12h this is common post-cardiac surgery, so predictive value is limited.

Persistent insulin infusion into 13–24h indicates ongoing severe hyperglycemia/critical illness, which is more informative for downstream organ dysfunction including AKI. Still indirect and confounded by ICU glycemic targets.
Insulin infusion is an indirect marker of stress hyperglycemia/critical illness severity, which can correlate with AKI risk, but it is not a direct renal perfusion or nephrotoxin signal.
Iodinated contrast can precipitate or worsen AKI, but in the immediate 0–12h post-op window effects may not yet be evident and exposure may be peri-procedural; capped by early-epoch conservatism.
Contrast exposure in the 13–24h trajectory phase is a recognized AKI risk factor and temporally closer to subsequent creatinine rise than immediate post-op exposure.
Contrast exposure in the 25–36h leading-indicator window is a strong, temporally proximate nephrotoxic insult that can directly contribute to creatinine rise by hour 60.
Inhaled ipratropium has no meaningful direct renal toxicity and mainly reflects pulmonary management; early post-op use is common and nonspecific for AKI.
Continued bronchodilator use is not mechanistically linked to AKI and is more reflective of respiratory status than renal perfusion or nephrotoxin exposure.
Persistent need for inhaled anticholinergic may weakly proxy ongoing critical illness, but it is not an established predictor of postoperative AKI.
Inhaled bronchodilators are not direct AKI drivers; early post-op administration is routine and not specific for renal injury risk.
Ongoing bronchodilator dosing primarily reflects pulmonary disease/ventilator course and has minimal direct relationship to creatinine rise.
May weakly indicate persistent respiratory compromise and overall acuity, but lacks a direct nephrotoxic or renal perfusion mechanism.
Iron administration is typically related to anemia management and does not directly predict AKI; early dosing is nonspecific.
Iron therapy is not an established AKI risk factor and more reflects transfusion/anemia strategy than renal injury physiology.
Late iron use may weakly proxy ongoing anemia/bleeding management and illness severity, but has no strong direct link to AKI.
Nitrates can lower preload/BP and could indirectly affect renal perfusion, but early post-op use is often protocolized and effects are transient; capped by 0–12h conservatism.
Ongoing nitrate use may indicate ischemia/heart failure management and potential hypotension risk, which can contribute indirectly to AKI, but it is not a primary predictor.

In the leading-indicator window, nitrate use may reflect persistent cardiac dysfunction and/or hypotension-prone management that can reduce renal perfusion; still an indirect marker rather than a definitive AKI signal.

Phosphate replacement may reflect hypophosphatemia from shifts/insulin or nutrition rather than kidney injury; early post-op electrolyte repletion is common. Indirect link at best and in the immediate 0–12h window is often transient.
Ongoing phosphate replacement can correlate with severity of illness and evolving renal dysfunction, but it is not a direct AKI marker. Still largely a supportive-care signal rather than mechanism.
Later need for phosphate replacement may track with persistent critical illness and possible renal handling abnormalities, closer to the outcome window. Remains indirect and non-specific for AKI.

Ketamine is not a typical nephrotoxin and dosing mainly reflects analgesia/sedation strategy. Any association with AKI would be confounded by illness severity and hemodynamics, especially in the immediate post-op period.
Continued ketamine use may indicate ongoing ventilatory support or agitation/pain, serving as a weak proxy for acuity. It has no strong direct physiologic link to renal tubular injury.
Later ketamine dosing may reflect prolonged ICU course and severity, but remains non-specific and not an established AKI driver. Kept low to avoid over-weighting sedation practice variation.

Early crystalloid volume can contribute to fluid overload/hemodilution and reflect resuscitation needs, but 0–12h post-cardiac surgery fluids are routine and often transient. Weak-to-plausible indirect relationship with AKI risk.

Ongoing crystalloid requirement in 13–24h may indicate persistent vasoplegia/low effective circulating volume, which can impair renal perfusion. Still indirect and confounded by overall resuscitation strategy.

Continued fluid administration in 25–36h can signal ongoing hemodynamic instability or third-spacing and may worsen renal congestion/fluid overload, both associated with AKI after cardiac surgery. Closer temporal proximity makes it more informative.
Lactulose use is typically for constipation or hepatic encephalopathy and is not a direct AKI predictor. In the immediate post-op window it is largely routine and non-specific.
Lactulose administration mainly reflects bowel regimen practices; any AKI association would be indirect via diarrhea/volume depletion and is uncommon in this setting. Kept minimal for sparsity.
Later lactulose could contribute to GI losses and relative hypovolemia, which can worsen renal perfusion, but this is an inconsistent and indirect pathway. Low importance due to non-specificity.
Levothyroxine dosing reflects chronic hypothyroidism management and is not an acute driver of postoperative AKI. Early post-op administration is routine continuation of home meds.
Continuation of thyroid replacement has no meaningful short-term effect on creatinine trajectory over 60 hours. Any signal would be confounded and weak.
Levothyroxine remains unrelated to acute renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity in this timeframe. Included only as a very low-probability covariate.
Topical lidocaine is an analgesic adjunct with negligible systemic nephrotoxic effect. Use reflects pain-control preference rather than renal risk, especially in 0–12h.
Topical lidocaine is minimally systemic and not nephrotoxic; dose mainly reflects pain management rather than renal perfusion or injury.
Topical lidocaine exposure has little physiologic impact on kidney function; more a marker of analgesia needs than AKI risk.
Systemic lidocaine is not a typical driver of AKI; early postoperative dosing is common and more related to arrhythmia/pain protocols than renal injury (conservative in 0–12h).
Lidocaine is not a recognized nephrotoxin and usually does not meaningfully alter renal perfusion; may weakly proxy illness/arrhythmia burden.
Ongoing systemic lidocaine may indicate persistent arrhythmias/instability, which can correlate with AKI risk, but the drug itself is not a direct renal insult.

ACE inhibitors can reduce intraglomerular pressure and worsen creatinine in low-perfusion states, but are often held immediately post-cardiac surgery; early dosing is uncommon and confounded (cap in 0–12h).
ACE inhibitor exposure can contribute to creatinine rise when renal perfusion is marginal; however, use may also reflect clinical stability, limiting specificity.
In the 25–36h window, ACE inhibitor dosing can more plausibly influence subsequent creatinine via efferent arteriolar dilation, especially with hypovolemia/low cardiac output.
ARBs can precipitate functional AKI in the setting of perioperative hypotension/volume depletion, but are typically withheld early post-op and thus are a weak, confounded signal (0–12h cap).
ARB exposure may contribute to creatinine increase when renal perfusion is compromised; still not a definitive predictor because administration often implies hemodynamic stability.
Closer to the outcome window, ARB dosing can meaningfully affect creatinine via reduced intraglomerular pressure in susceptible patients, though it remains an indirect predictor.
Magnesium sulfate is commonly given for electrolyte repletion/arrhythmia prophylaxis and is not a typical cause of AKI; early dosing is routine and nonspecific.
Ongoing magnesium sulfate use mainly reflects electrolyte management; only a weak indirect link to AKI via overall illness severity or renal clearance changes.
Later magnesium sulfate dosing may weakly proxy reduced renal clearance or continued ICU complexity, but it is not a direct renal injury marker.
Magnesium replacement is standard post-cardiac surgery and not nephrotoxic; early administration provides little specific information about impending AKI.
Continued magnesium dosing is a weak, indirect signal that may reflect electrolyte derangements or reduced clearance, but is not an established AKI predictor.
Magnesium replacement is common post-cardiac surgery and usually reflects electrolyte management rather than renal injury; accumulation can occur with AKI but dosing is an indirect signal.
Melatonin use is primarily for sleep/delirium prevention and has no established mechanistic link to AKI risk; early postoperative use is routine and nonspecific.
Continued melatonin dosing reflects sleep management rather than renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity and is not a recognized AKI predictor.
Melatonin remains a nonspecific supportive medication with minimal direct relationship to subsequent creatinine rise.
Broad-spectrum antibiotics can be a proxy for severe infection/instability, which increases AKI risk, but meropenem itself is not strongly nephrotoxic; early epoch is capped conservatively.
Ongoing meropenem suggests suspected/treated infection or complications that can drive AKI via sepsis and hemodynamic stress, but remains an indirect marker.
Meropenem use closer to outcome may indicate persistent infection/critical illness (a known AKI driver), though the drug is not a definitive nephrotoxin; kept at indirect-link cap.
Methocarbamol is used for muscle spasm/pain control and has no meaningful direct association with renal hypoperfusion or tubular injury; early use is nonspecific.
Analgesic adjunct use does not reliably track renal physiology or AKI risk and is not an established predictor.
Continued methocarbamol dosing is unlikely to add prognostic information for creatinine rise beyond overall illness severity.
Methylene blue use often indicates vasoplegic shock after cardiopulmonary bypass, a hemodynamic state strongly linked to renal hypoperfusion; early epoch capped at 0.10.
Need for methylene blue in the trajectory phase suggests persistent/refractory vasoplegia and high vasopressor burden, which increases AKI risk via sustained hypoperfusion.
Late methylene blue use implies ongoing severe vasoplegia/hemodynamic collapse close to the outcome window, a strong direct predictor of subsequent creatinine rise.
Metoclopramide is used for nausea/gastroparesis and is not a recognized driver or marker of AKI; early postoperative administration is routine.
Antiemetic/prokinetic use provides little specific information about renal perfusion, nephrotoxicity, or evolving AKI.
Continued metoclopramide dosing remains nonspecific and unlikely to improve prediction of creatinine_ratio in a sparse linear model.

Beta-blocker dose is not a direct AKI driver; early post-op dosing mainly reflects rate control and hemodynamic tolerance. In the immediate 0–12h period, transient instability is common, so prognostic value for later creatinine rise is limited.
Ongoing metoprolol use may indirectly reflect cardiovascular stability (less shock/vasopressor need), which can relate to renal perfusion. Still an indirect marker and not a primary AKI predictor.

Later metoprolol dosing can be a weak proxy for hemodynamic stability and controlled arrhythmias, which may support renal perfusion. Association is indirect and likely small compared with direct hemodynamic/renal markers.
Metronidazole is not typically nephrotoxic and is unlikely to causally affect creatinine trajectory. Use in 0–12h mostly reflects infection/prophylaxis decisions rather than renal injury.

Antibiotic selection may weakly proxy suspected infection/abdominal source, which can contribute to systemic inflammation and AKI risk. Metronidazole itself has minimal direct renal toxicity, so relevance remains low.
Continued metronidazole may indicate evolving infection/sepsis risk, which can precipitate AKI via hypotension/inflammation. Still an indirect signal and not a direct biomarker.
Sedation dose is not a direct cause of AKI; it mainly reflects ventilator management and agitation. Early post-op sedation is routine and poorly specific for later creatinine rise.

Higher ongoing sedation can correlate with illness severity, prolonged ventilation, and hemodynamic instability, which may increase AKI risk. This is an indirect severity marker with limited specificity.
Persistent midazolam requirement later may reflect ongoing critical illness and need for mechanical ventilation, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Not a mechanistic renal predictor, so kept low.

Midodrine use suggests vasoplegia or difficulty weaning IV vasopressors, indirectly indicating compromised perfusion pressure relevant to AKI. In 0–12h, however, vasoplegia is common and transient, limiting predictive strength.

Need for midodrine in the trajectory phase can indicate persistent hypotension/vasodilatory shock physiology, which threatens renal perfusion and increases AKI risk. Still indirect compared with direct MAP/vasopressor dose measures.

Midodrine requirement close to the outcome window suggests ongoing low effective arterial blood volume or vasoplegia, a clinically meaningful risk state for renal hypoperfusion and AKI. This is a stronger leading indicator than earlier epochs but remains a proxy.

Milrinone use indicates low cardiac output/right ventricular dysfunction management, which can reduce renal perfusion and precipitate AKI. In the immediate post-op period, inotrope use is common, so predictive value is moderated by the 0–12h cap.

Ongoing milrinone suggests persistent cardiac dysfunction and/or pulmonary hypertension with risk of renal hypoperfusion and venous congestion, both linked to AKI. This is an established hemodynamic risk factor in the trajectory phase.

Continued milrinone late (25–36h) strongly signals unresolved low-output state or RV failure, which directly compromises renal perfusion and promotes cardiorenal AKI. Proximity to hour-60 creatinine makes it a strong predictor.

Minocycline is not a typical nephrotoxin in this context and is unlikely to drive postoperative creatinine changes. Early administration mainly reflects antimicrobial choice rather than renal pathophysiology.
Minocycline is not a typical peri-cardiac surgery nephrotoxin and is rarely a primary driver of AKI risk. Dose mainly reflects infection treatment rather than renal perfusion/injury.
Minocycline exposure has weak and indirect linkage to postoperative AKI compared with hemodynamics and renal labs. More likely a marker of infection management than kidney injury mechanism.

Opioid dosing can correlate with illness severity and hypotension/respiratory depression, but it is not a direct AKI driver. In the immediate 0–12h post-op window, dosing is common and less discriminative.
Ongoing opioid requirement may reflect persistent instability or complications that can secondarily affect renal perfusion. Still an indirect proxy rather than a direct renal insult.
Higher opioid needs late may indicate ongoing critical illness (e.g., prolonged ventilation, shock) that can associate with AKI risk. Remains indirect and confounded by care practices.

Nicardipine use reflects postoperative hypertension management; excessive BP lowering could reduce renal perfusion, but early post-op use is common and transient. Thus it is a weak-to-indirect AKI predictor in 0–12h.
Continued antihypertensive infusion may indicate persistent hemodynamic lability and potential renal perfusion vulnerability. Association is plausible but not specific for AKI.

Late need for nicardipine may capture sustained BP management issues where overly low MAP or wide swings can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Still an indirect marker without direct renal specificity.

Nitroglycerin can lower preload/BP and is used for ischemia/hypertension; hypotension can worsen renal perfusion, but early use is common post-cardiac surgery. Therefore predictive value for AKI is limited in 0–12h.
Ongoing nitrate infusion may indicate persistent cardiac ischemia/heart failure physiology with potential low effective renal perfusion. It is a plausible but nonspecific AKI risk correlate.
Late nitroglycerin requirement can reflect ongoing cardiac dysfunction or BP management that may compromise renal perfusion. This is an indirect hemodynamic proxy rather than a direct biomarker.

Early crystalloid volume relates to resuscitation and hemodynamics; both hypovolemia and fluid overload can affect renal outcomes, but 0–12h volumes are heavily protocol-driven. Hence only modest, indirect predictive value.
Continued saline administration may reflect ongoing resuscitation needs or poor perfusion, which can associate with AKI risk. Still confounded by practice patterns and other fluids/diuretics.

Late ongoing crystalloid requirement suggests persistent hemodynamic issues or third spacing; both can precede AKI and rising creatinine by hour 60. Volume strategy can influence renal congestion/perfusion, making this a stronger (though still indirect) predictor.
Nystatin is minimally systemically absorbed and not nephrotoxic; it does not meaningfully affect renal perfusion or tubular injury. Use is routine prophylaxis/treatment and not AKI-informative.
Nystatin exposure has no established mechanistic link to postoperative AKI. It mainly reflects oral/GI antifungal prophylaxis rather than renal risk.
Nystatin is minimally systemically absorbed and is not a typical nephrotoxin. Use mainly reflects routine antifungal prophylaxis rather than renal perfusion or injury.
Ondansetron has no meaningful direct link to AKI risk; dosing reflects nausea prophylaxis. In the immediate 0–12h post-op period it is especially nonspecific.
Antiemetic use is not a physiologic driver of renal injury and is weakly related to AKI at best. More likely reflects comfort care needs than kidney perfusion.
Continued ondansetron use is not an established predictor of creatinine rise. It is a nonspecific marker of symptoms rather than renal pathophysiology.
Opioid dosing may indirectly correlate with illness severity and hypotension/sedation, but it is not a primary AKI driver. Early post-op analgesia is common and nonspecific.
Higher opioid needs can be a weak proxy for ongoing stress/instability that may accompany AKI risk, but the link is indirect. Not a direct renal biomarker.
Persistent opioid requirement may track with prolonged critical illness and reduced mobility/PO intake, indirectly associated with AKI. Still not a direct mechanism or established predictor.
Pantoprazole is routine stress-ulcer prophylaxis and provides little specific information about renal injury risk. PPI-associated interstitial nephritis is rare and not an acute 60h driver.
Continued PPI use is standard ICU care and not informative for AKI prediction in this timeframe. Any renal association is weak and typically delayed.
Pantoprazole dosing remains a nonspecific maintenance therapy with minimal short-term AKI signal. Not a direct marker of renal hypoperfusion or tubular injury.
