Need for vasopressor support indicates perioperative vasoplegia/hypotension, which can reduce renal perfusion and contribute to AKI. However, in 0–12h this is common and transient, so importance is capped conservatively.
Ongoing vasopressor requirement in the trajectory phase suggests persistent hemodynamic instability and renal hypoperfusion risk. This is a more informative signal than immediate post-op use.

Vasopressor need close to the outcome window is a strong indicator of sustained shock/low effective renal perfusion, a key driver of postoperative AKI. This is a clinically meaningful leading indicator for creatinine rise.
Phosphate replacement suggests hypophosphatemia from shifts/insulin/CPB effects and is not a direct AKI marker. In 0–12h it is common and nonspecific.
Continued phosphate replacement may weakly reflect ongoing metabolic derangements or nutrition/renal handling changes, but the link to AKI is indirect. Not an established predictor.

Later phosphate replacement can be a weak proxy for evolving renal dysfunction or critical illness, but it is not a direct biomarker and remains nonspecific. Kept below 0.05 to avoid over-weighting behavioral/management signals.

Broad-spectrum antibiotic use is more a marker of suspected infection/acuity than a direct AKI driver; piperacillin-tazobactam has limited intrinsic nephrotoxicity. Early (0–12h) dosing is also less temporally proximate to the 60h creatinine outcome.

Ongoing need for broad-spectrum antibiotics may reflect evolving sepsis/inflammation that can contribute to AKI risk, but the drug itself is not a strong direct nephrotoxin. Kept low to reflect indirect linkage and avoid over-weighting treatment-intensity proxies.

Antibiotic continuation closer to the outcome can indicate persistent infection/vasoplegia risk, which can worsen renal perfusion. Still primarily a behavioral/acuity marker rather than a definitive renal biomarker, so capped <0.05.

Crystalloid volume reflects resuscitation needs and can correlate with hemodynamic instability, but is not a specific AKI mechanism on its own. In the immediate post-op window, fluid administration is common and often transient.

Continued crystalloid requirement may indicate ongoing hypoperfusion/vasoplegia and can contribute to renal congestion via positive balance, but remains nonspecific. Scored low to reflect indirect association.

Later crystalloid needs may better capture persistent shock or attempts to treat low urine output, both linked to AKI risk. Still a nonspecific treatment variable and potentially collinear with other hemodynamic/volume features, so kept at the indirect-link cap.
Polyethylene glycol is a bowel regimen and has no meaningful physiologic link to postoperative AKI. Use mainly reflects routine ICU care rather than renal risk.
Bowel regimen dosing is not a driver or marker of renal hypoperfusion or tubular injury. It is standard supportive care with minimal prognostic value for creatinine rise.
Continued laxative use does not indicate kidney injury risk and is largely unrelated to creatinine trajectory. Kept at minimal importance for sparsity.

Potassium repletion can indirectly signal renal dysfunction (reduced excretion) or shifts from acidosis/hemolysis, but early post-op electrolyte management is common. As a treatment proxy with mixed directionality, it is kept low and conservative in 0–12h.

Ongoing potassium supplementation may reflect diuretic use, renal losses, or clinician response to arrhythmia risk; it is an indirect marker of physiologic instability. Association with AKI is plausible but nonspecific, so capped at 0.05.

Later potassium dosing may better reflect evolving renal handling and ongoing diuretic/acid-base management closer to the creatinine outcome. Still indirect and potentially confounded by treatment decisions, so kept modest.
Dexmedetomidine is not nephrotoxic; any AKI relationship is indirect via sedation depth and potential bradycardia/hypotension. In the immediate post-op period it is routine and weakly informative.

Continued sedation may indicate prolonged ventilation/critical illness, which correlates with AKI risk, but this is an indirect severity marker. Kept low to avoid over-weighting non-renal treatment variables.

Sedation requirement closer to 60h can reflect ongoing instability and prolonged ICU course, indirectly associated with AKI. Still not a direct renal biomarker and may be collinear with ventilation/hemodynamic features, so <0.05.

Prednisone use is not a direct cause of AKI in this setting; it more often reflects chronic therapy or specific inflammatory indications. Early dosing has weak and nonspecific linkage to postoperative creatinine rise.

Systemic steroids can contribute to hyperglycemia and fluid shifts, which may indirectly worsen renal risk, but prednisone is not a typical direct driver of post-cardiac surgery AKI. Trajectory-phase timing supports only a small weight.

Ongoing steroid exposure closer to the outcome may reflect inflammatory complications and can worsen glycemic control, indirectly affecting AKI risk. Still not a primary nephrotoxin or direct renal biomarker, so kept low.

Higher propofol dosing may correlate with deeper sedation and hypotension/vasodilation early after surgery, indirectly affecting renal perfusion. In the immediate 0–12h period derangements are common and transient, so conservative scoring.

Continued propofol requirement may indicate ongoing mechanical ventilation and hemodynamic fragility, which can be associated with AKI risk. It is an indirect marker rather than a direct renal insult.

Persistent sedation needs in the 25–36h window can proxy ongoing critical illness and potential hypotension affecting kidney perfusion. Propofol itself is not strongly nephrotoxic, so capped at plausible-indirect.

Protamine use is routine after cardiopulmonary bypass and mainly reflects standard reversal of heparin rather than renal injury. Rare reactions can cause hypotension, but overall predictive value for AKI is weak.

Protamine administration beyond the immediate post-bypass period is uncommon and more likely reflects procedural workflow than kidney pathophysiology. Limited direct linkage to subsequent creatinine rise.
Late protamine exposure is unlikely to be a causal or consistent predictor of AKI and may represent idiosyncratic care events. Minimal expected relationship to creatinine_ratio.
Quetiapine is used for agitation/delirium and does not directly affect renal perfusion or cause typical nephrotoxicity in this setting. Any association would be confounding by illness severity.
Use may indicate delirium and prolonged ICU course, indirectly correlating with complications, but it is not a mechanistic AKI driver. Kept very low to maintain sparsity.

Ongoing antipsychotic use may proxy persistent delirium/critical illness closer to outcome, but remains an indirect and nonspecific marker. Not expected to add strong independent signal for creatinine_ratio.

Neuromuscular blockade reflects ventilation management and does not directly cause AKI; any link is via severity of illness and ventilation needs. Early post-op period is highly confounded, so conservative.

Continued paralysis may indicate severe respiratory failure or ventilator dyssynchrony, indirectly associated with hemodynamic instability and AKI risk. Still nonspecific and not a direct renal biomarker.

Persistent neuromuscular blockade in the 25–36h window can signal ongoing critical illness and potential shock/ventilation severity that can impair renal perfusion. Indirect association only, so kept below 0.05.
Statin continuation is routine and not a typical acute driver of post-cardiac surgery AKI; rhabdomyolysis is rare and unlikely to be captured by dose alone. Minimal expected predictive value.
Ongoing statin dosing mainly reflects baseline therapy continuation rather than evolving renal injury. Weak and likely confounded relationship to creatinine_ratio.

Statins are not typical direct drivers of early post-cardiac surgery AKI; dose variation mainly reflects chronic lipid therapy continuation. Rhabdomyolysis-related AKI is rare and unlikely captured by routine rosuvastatin dosing.
Bowel regimen use is routine ICU care and has no meaningful physiologic link to renal perfusion or tubular injury. Early post-op administration is more workflow-related than AKI-related.
Senna/docusate dosing reflects constipation prophylaxis and nursing practice rather than kidney injury risk. No established association with creatinine rise.
Continued bowel regimen is not a marker of hemodynamic instability or nephrotoxin exposure. Minimal relevance for predicting creatinine_ratio.
Senna is standard supportive care and does not directly affect renal function in this context. Any dehydration effect is unlikely in tightly managed ICU fluid protocols.
Senna dosing is not an established AKI predictor and mainly reflects routine prophylaxis. Weak indirect link at most.
Bowel stimulant use does not capture renal hypoperfusion, congestion, or nephrotoxic exposure. Very low expected predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Sevelamer use suggests hyperphosphatemia and/or advanced CKD, which increases susceptibility to postoperative creatinine rise. In the immediate 0–12h window, it may still reflect pre-existing disease and routine continuation, so kept low.
Ongoing sevelamer use can indicate persistent hyperphosphatemia/CKD and higher baseline renal vulnerability. It is an indirect marker rather than a causal driver of AKI.

In the 25–36h leading window, sevelamer may reflect evolving phosphate retention from worsening renal function or known CKD, both associated with higher creatinine_ratio. Still indirect and treatment-driven, so capped at plausible-indirect range.
Simethicone is a benign GI medication with no physiologic connection to renal perfusion or tubular injury. Use is routine and not informative for AKI risk.
Simethicone dosing reflects GI comfort measures and does not track hemodynamics, congestion, or nephrotoxins. No meaningful expected association with creatinine_ratio.
Continued simethicone use is unrelated to kidney injury mechanisms and is unlikely to add predictive signal. Kept near-zero for sparsity.

Bicarbonate administration can indicate metabolic acidosis, hyperkalemia, or resuscitation needs, which correlate with shock and AKI risk. In 0–12h many derangements are transient post-CPB, so conservatively scored under the epoch cap.

Need for bicarbonate in 13–24h suggests persistent acidosis or ongoing hypoperfusion/low cardiac output, established contributors to AKI. This is a treatment proxy for severity rather than a direct biomarker, so kept below 0.20.

Bicarbonate requirement close to the outcome window often reflects sustained metabolic acidosis from shock, renal dysfunction, or severe illness, all strongly linked to subsequent creatinine rise. It is a strong severity marker in the 25–36h leading indicator period.

TPN use is not a direct driver of early post-cardiac surgery AKI and mainly reflects nutrition/illness complexity. In the immediate 0–12h window it is unlikely to add specific signal for creatinine rise by 60h.
Ongoing TPN may proxy higher acuity or inability to feed, which can correlate with complications, but it is not mechanistically linked to AKI. Kept low to maintain sparsity.
TPN at 25–36h may indicate persistent critical illness and inflammatory stress, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Still a weak, non-specific predictor compared with hemodynamics/renal labs.
Tamsulosin is not nephrotoxic and has minimal relevance to renal perfusion in the ICU setting. Use early post-op is more related to urinary retention management than AKI biology.
Tamsulosin dosing does not meaningfully predict intrinsic renal injury; it is largely unrelated to postoperative creatinine trajectory. Any association would be incidental.
Continued tamsulosin reflects lower urinary tract management rather than renal function decline. Not expected to add predictive value for creatinine_ratio.
Tramadol is not a classic nephrotoxin; any link to AKI would be indirect via hypotension/sedation or overall pain/illness severity. In 0–12h, dosing is heavily protocol-driven.
Higher analgesic needs can weakly proxy complications or prolonged ventilation, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Still non-specific and kept low for sparsity.
Ongoing tramadol use may reflect persistent pain/immobility and overall acuity, which can correlate with AKI, but it is not a direct causal factor. Low importance to avoid overfitting.

Tranexamic acid is primarily related to bleeding control; AKI associations are inconsistent and largely confounded by surgical complexity. In the immediate post-op window, it is not a strong renal predictor.
Use beyond the OR/early period is uncommon and may reflect bleeding risk rather than renal injury mechanisms. Any AKI signal is indirect and likely confounded.
Late tranexamic acid use would mainly indicate ongoing bleeding management and complexity, not direct nephrotoxicity. Kept very low due to weak mechanistic linkage to creatinine rise.

Vancomycin exposure is a recognized nephrotoxic risk factor, but early dosing often reflects empiric coverage rather than established toxicity. Conservative scoring due to 0–12h epoch cap and delayed toxicity.

Continued vancomycin increases nephrotoxic exposure and may reflect infection/sepsis risk, both associated with AKI. Moderate importance but capped to avoid redundancy and because toxicity is dose/level dependent.

Vancomycin exposure closer to the outcome window is more likely to contribute to or mark evolving AKI, especially in elderly post-cardiac surgery patients. Still not definitive without trough levels, so not higher.

Need for vasopressin indicates vasoplegia/shock and impaired renal perfusion risk, an established driver of postoperative AKI. Scored conservatively due to the immediate 0–12h period where transient pressor use is common.

Ongoing vasopressin requirement suggests persistent vasoplegia/shock and renal hypoperfusion risk, which is associated with AKI after cardiac surgery. Mid-epoch signal is informative but less proximal than 25–36h.

Late vasopressin requirement is a strong marker of ongoing shock/vasoplegia and high illness severity close to the creatinine outcome window, increasing AKI risk. More proximal hemodynamic support needs are more predictive.
Warfarin dosing early post-op is not a direct driver of AKI and is often protocol/indication driven rather than reflecting renal perfusion. Any association would be indirect and weak.
Warfarin use reflects anticoagulation strategy rather than kidney injury physiology in this timeframe. It has no established short-term causal link to postoperative creatinine rise.
Warfarin dose is unlikely to predict creatinine ratio at 60h except via confounding by indication; it is not a typical AKI trigger in the immediate postoperative ICU course. Keep low for sparsity.
Heparin line flush dosing is routine ICU maintenance and does not meaningfully affect renal perfusion or tubular injury. Any signal would be noise or documentation-related.
Line flush heparin is standard care and not a physiologic predictor of AKI. It mainly reflects line management rather than kidney risk.
Heparin flush dose remains a non-informative maintenance variable with no direct AKI mechanism. Cap very low to avoid spurious inclusion.

Early oliguria can indicate renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI, but immediate post-bypass diuresis/fluids and transient hemodynamic shifts make this window less specific. Capped at the 0–12h maximum.

Urine output in the trajectory phase reflects whether renal function is recovering or worsening after initial surgical insult. Persistent low output is a strong early functional marker for subsequent creatinine rise.

Low urine output close to hour 60 is a direct functional indicator of impaired renal filtration and is strongly predictive of subsequent creatinine elevation. This is one of the most clinically actionable early AKI signals.
High early vasopressor needs suggest perioperative shock/low renal perfusion, but immediate post-op vasoplegia is common and may be transient. Conservative scoring due to low specificity in 0–12h.

Continued vasopressor requirement indicates ongoing hemodynamic instability and risk of renal ischemia, which is associated with AKI after cardiac surgery. More informative than the immediate post-op period but less than 25–36h.

Late, sustained vasopressor requirement is a strong marker of persistent shock and renal hypoperfusion near the outcome window, increasing likelihood of creatinine rise. This is a high-value hemodynamic predictor for AKI.
